topics.title: Image Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Image Representation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **digital image** is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ that has been digitized in both **spatial coordinates** $(x, y)$ and **intensity (brightness) amplitude** $f$. When the spatial coordinates and the amplitude values are all finite and discrete quantities, the image is called a **digital image**. Mathematically, a digital image can be represented as a finite two-dimensional matrix of intensity values: $$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} f(0, 0) & f(0, 1) & \cdots & f(0, N-1) \\ f(1, 0) & f(1, 1) & \cdots & f(1, N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1, 0) & f(M-1, 1) & \cdots & f(M-1, N-1) \end{bmatrix}$$ Where: - $M$ = number of rows (image height in pixels) - $N$ = number of columns (image width in pixels) - $M \times N$ = total number of **pixels (picture elements)** - $f(x, y)$ = intensity (gray level) at pixel location $(x, y)$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pixel (Picture Element / Pel):** The smallest addressable element of a digital image, holding a single integer value representing light intensity or color at that spatial location. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A digital image originates from optical phenomena in nature. The function $f(x, y)$ is determined by two physical components: > 1. The **amount of illumination** incident on the scene. > 2. The **amount of illumination reflected** by the objects in the scene. These two components combine as: $$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$ Where: - $i(x, y)$ = illumination component, with $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$ - $r(x, y)$ = reflectance component, with $0 < r(x, y) < 1$ --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > **The Mosaic Wall Analogy** > > Imagine a giant wall built from thousands of tiny coloured tiles. Stand far away, and the wall looks like a smooth painting (a continuous-tone photograph). Walk close enough, and you see that the "image" is nothing but a **grid of discrete coloured squares**. Each tile is a **pixel**, and its colour value is its **intensity**. The arrangement of all tiles is the **matrix representation** of the image. The wall's overall sharpness depends on how many tiles you used (resolution), and how many distinct colours each tile can hold (bit depth). ### Coordinate Convention used in KTU / Gonzalez & Woods | Convention | Origin | Row Index | Column Index | Reading Direction | |------------|--------|-----------|--------------|-------------------| | Standard (Image Processing) | Top-Left | $y$ (downward) | $x$ (rightward) | Left to Right, Top to Bottom | | Mathematical (Matrix) | Top-Left or Bottom-Left | Row $m$ | Column $n$ | Either | > [!TIP] > In **MATLAB / DIP textbooks**, the origin $(0, 0)$ is at the **top-left** of the image, the $x$-axis points **right**, and the $y$-axis points **down**. This is opposite to the standard Cartesian plane and frequently confuses students in examinations. --- ## 1.3 Categories of Digital Images > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Favourite Classification — Three Principal Image Types:** 1. **Binary Image** — Pixels take only two values: $\{0, 1\}$. Requires **1 bit/pixel**. Used in document scanning, masks, and morphological operations. 2. **Grayscale Image** — Pixels take integer intensity values in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$ where $L = 2^k$ and $k$ is the number of bits per pixel. The standard $k=8$ gives 256 gray levels. 3. **Colour Image** — Each pixel is a vector of three (or more) channels. In the **RGB model**, each pixel has three components $(R, G, B)$. Total bits per pixel = $3k$, typically $3 \times 8 = 24$ bits. --- ## 1.4 Key Metrics & Standard Constants (Bolded for Board Exams) - **Pixel:** Smallest addressable element of an image. - **Resolution:** Degree of discernible detail; expressed in pixels per unit length. - **Dynamic Range:** Ratio of maximum measurable intensity to minimum measurable intensity. - **Contrast:** Difference in intensity between an object and its background. - **Spatial Resolution:** Density of pixels per unit area (e.g., **dots per inch (DPI)**). - **Gray-Level (Intensity) Resolution:** Smallest discernible change in intensity level, determined by the number of bits $k$. - **Saturation:** Maximum intensity value a sensor can record. - **Noise:** Random variation in pixel values. > [!NOTE] > **Image Formation Model (KTU Module 1 Anchor Concept):** > The two physical variables $i(x,y)$ and $r(x,y)$ together form the basis of the **image formation model**. While $i(x,y)$ is determined by the illumination source (e.g., sunlight, indoor lamp), $r(x,y)$ is determined by the physical nature of the object's surface (e.g., black velvet has $r \approx 0$, stainless steel has $r \approx 1$). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Components of the Image Function $f(x, y)$ The digital image $f(x, y)$ is decomposed into two physically meaningful multiplicative components: $$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$ ### 2.1.1 Illumination Component $i(x, y)$ - Determined entirely by the **light source** (natural or artificial). - Range: $\mathbf{0 < i(x, y) < \infty}$. - For typical indoor scenes: $i(x, y) \approx 100$ lux (denoted $\text{lx}$). - For sunny outdoor scenes: $i(x, y) \approx 1000$ lux. - For overcast outdoor scenes: $i(x, y) \approx 10000$ lux. ### 2.1.2 Reflectance Component $r(x, y)$ - Determined by the **physical and chemical properties** of the object's surface. - Range: $\mathbf{0 < r(x, y) < 1}$ (theoretically). - **Black velvet:** $r(x, y) \approx 0.01$ (absorbs nearly all light). - **Snow:** $r(x, y) \approx 0.93$ (reflects nearly all light). - **Stainless steel:** $r(x, y) \approx 0.65$. - **Flat black paint:** $r(x, y) \approx 0.04$. > [!NOTE] > The value $f(x, y) = 0$ is interpreted as **black**, and $f(x, y) = L - 1$ (the maximum) is interpreted as **white** on the grayscale. All intermediate values represent shades of gray. --- ## 2.2 Sampling, Quantization & Bit Depth A continuous image $f(x, y)$ must be converted to a digital form using two operations: 1. **Sampling** — Digitizing the spatial coordinates $(x, y)$. Produces a discrete grid of $M \times N$ samples. 2. **Quantization** — Digitizing the intensity amplitude $f$. Produces $L$ discrete gray levels. The relationship between the number of gray levels $L$ and the number of bits $k$ used per pixel is: $$L = 2^k$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this formula critical?** > Board examiners consistently award 2–3 marks simply for stating the relationship $L = 2^k$ and its inverse $k = \log_2 L$. Memorize this. For a standard 8-bit image, $L = 2^8 = 256$ levels. --- ## 2.3 Storage Requirement (Bits Required to Store an Image) The number of bits $b$ required to store an $M \times N$ image with $k$ bits per pixel is: $$b = M \times N \times k$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When a question asks "How much memory is required to store a $1024 \times 1024$ 8-bit grayscale image?" — students often forget to convert bits to **bytes, KB, MB, or GB**. Always show unit conversion: > $$\text{Memory} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8} \text{ bytes}$$ --- ## 2.4 Spatial Resolution vs. Gray-Level Resolution | Property | Spatial Resolution | Gray-Level Resolution | |----------|-------------------|----------------------| | Definition | Smallest discernible detail in **space** | Smallest discernible change in **intensity** | | Controlled by | Number of pixels $M \times N$ | Number of bits per pixel $k$ | | Unit | **Dots per inch (DPI)**, lines per mm | Number of distinguishable gray levels $L$ | | Effect of decrease | Pixels become visible (blocky/pixelated) | False contouring / banding appears | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students frequently use the terms "resolution" and "image size" interchangeably. They are **different**: > - **Image size** = $M \times N$ (number of pixels). > - **Spatial resolution** = $M \times N$ per unit area (density). --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Image function | $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | $i$: illumination, $r$: reflectance | | 2 | Illumination range | $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$ | In lux (lx) | | 3 | Reflectance range | $0 < r(x, y) < 1$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^k$ | $k$ = bits per pixel | | 5 | Bits per pixel | $k = \log_2 L$ | Inverse of #4 | | 6 | Storage in bits | $b = M \times N \times k$ | Total bits | | 7 | Storage in bytes | $b = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8}$ | Divide by 8 | | 8 | Storage in KB | $b = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024}$ | Divide by 8192 | | 9 | Storage in MB | $b = \dfrac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024}$ | Divide by 8388608 | | 10 | Aspect ratio | $\text{AR} = \dfrac{N}{M}$ | Width / Height | | 11 | Dynamic range | $\text{DR} = \dfrac{f_{\max}}{f_{\min}}$ | Ratio | | 12 | Image diagonal | $d = \sqrt{M^2 + N^2}$ | In pixels | | 13 | Total pixels | $N_{\text{total}} = M \times N$ | Pixels | | 14 | DPI conversion | $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$ | Physical size | | 15 | Physical width | $W = \dfrac{N}{\text{DPI}}$ | In inches | --- ## 2.6 Color Image Representation — RGB Model In the **RGB (Red, Green, Blue) color model**, every color is represented as an additive combination of three primary components. The color at pixel $(x, y)$ is a 3-vector: $$\mathbf{C}(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} R(x, y) \\ G(x, y) \\ B(x, y) \end{bmatrix}$$ Each component is an 8-bit integer in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$ for a standard 24-bit color image. Total storage for an $M \times N$ RGB image: $$b_{\text{RGB}} = M \times N \times 24 \text{ bits} = M \times N \times 3 \text{ bytes}$$ | Color | $R$ | $G$ | $B$ | |-------|-----|-----|-----| | Black | 0 | 0 | 0 | | White | 255 | 255 | 255 | | Red | 255 | 0 | 0 | | Green | 0 | 255 | 0 | | Blue | 0 | 0 | 255 | | Yellow | 255 | 255 | 0 | | Cyan | 0 | 255 | 255 | | Magenta | 255 | 0 | 255 | --- ## 2.7 Other Color Models (Briefly, for KTU 2024 Awareness) | Model | Channels | Typical Use | |-------|----------|-------------| | **RGB** | Red, Green, Blue | Displays, cameras, scanners | | **HSI/HSV** | Hue, Saturation, Intensity/Value | Image processing (intuitive) | | **CMY / CMYK** | Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, Key (Black) | Printing | | **YCbCr** | Luma (Y), Blue-chroma (Cb), Red-chroma (Cr) | JPEG, video compression | | **Lab (CIE)** | Lightness (L), a*, b* | Perceptual uniformity | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Digital images are the bedrock of modern technology — from **medical imaging** (MRI, CT, X-ray) to **satellite remote sensing**, **biometric authentication** (fingerprint, iris, face), **autonomous vehicles** (lane detection, sign recognition), **industrial quality inspection**, and **social media content pipelines**. Every one of these systems begins with image representation, sampling, and quantization. --- ## 2.8 Vector vs. Scalar Image Notation For a grayscale image (scalar field): $$f: \mathbb{Z}^2 \to \mathbb{Z} \quad \text{such that} \quad f(x, y) \in \{0, 1, \dots, L-1\}$$ For a color image (vector field): $$\mathbf{F}: \mathbb{Z}^2 \to \mathbb{Z}^3 \quad \text{such that} \quad \mathbf{F}(x, y) = [R(x, y), G(x, y), B(x, y)]^T$$ # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Number of Gray Levels from Bit Depth **Problem:** Show that for a $k$-bit pixel, the number of distinguishable intensity levels is $L = 2^k$. **Step 1:** A $k$-bit binary number can take exactly $2^k$ distinct values. This is a fundamental property of the binary number system — each bit position contributes a factor of 2, and there are $k$ independent bit positions. $$\text{Number of distinct values} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{k \text{ times}} = 2^k$$ **Step 2:** The intensity $f$ is mapped one-to-one onto these binary values. Hence: $$L = 2^k$$ **Step 3:** For typical bit depths used in practice: | $k$ (bits) | $L = 2^k$ levels | Use case | |------------|------------------|----------| | 1 | 2 | Binary image (B&W) | | 2 | 4 | Low-quality graphics | | 4 | 16 | Old VGA modes | | 8 | 256 | Standard grayscale | | 12 | 4096 | Medical imaging | | 16 | 65536 | High-precision scientific | | 24 | 16777216 | Truecolor (RGB, $8 \times 3$ bits) | --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Requirement for an $M \times N$ Grayscale Image **Problem:** An image of size $M \times N$ is quantized to $k$ bits per pixel. Derive the total storage in bits, bytes, KB, and MB. **Step 1:** Total number of pixels: $$N_{\text{pixels}} = M \times N$$ This is because the image is an $M$-row by $N$-column rectangular grid, and each cell is one pixel. **Step 2:** Total storage in bits (one pixel = $k$ bits): $$b_{\text{bits}} = N_{\text{pixels}} \times k = M \times N \times k \quad \text{[bits]}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to bytes (1 byte = 8 bits): $$b_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8} \quad \text{[bytes]}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to kilobytes (1 KB = $1024$ bytes = $2^{10}$ bytes): $$b_{\text{KB}} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8192} \quad \text{[KB]}$$ **Step 5:** Convert to megabytes (1 MB = $1024 \times 1024 = 2^{20}$ bytes): $$b_{\text{MB}} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} = \frac{M \times N \times k}{8388608} \quad \text{[MB]}$$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Standard HD Grayscale Image **Given:** A $1920 \times 1080$ grayscale image with $k = 8$ bits/pixel. **Step 1:** Compute total pixels: $$M \times N = 1920 \times 1080 = 2073600 \text{ pixels}$$ **Step 2:** Compute total bits: $$b_{\text{bits}} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 8 = 16677600 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to bytes: $$b_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{16677600}{8} = 2084700 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to MB: $$b_{\text{MB}} = \frac{2084700}{1024 \times 1024} \approx 1.988 \text{ MB} \approx 1.99 \text{ MB}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Real-world observation:** A raw $1920 \times 1080$ 8-bit grayscale frame consumes about **2 MB**. This is why video compression standards (H.264, H.265) are essential — they reduce this by 50–100× for storage and transmission. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — RGB HD Image Storage **Given:** A $1920 \times 1080$ RGB color image with 8 bits per channel. **Step 1:** Total bits per pixel for RGB: $$k_{\text{RGB}} = 3 \times 8 = 24 \text{ bits/pixel}$$ **Step 2:** Total storage in bits: $$b_{\text{bits}} = 1920 \times 1080 \times 24 = 49766400 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to bytes: $$b_{\text{bytes}} = \frac{49766400}{8} = 6220800 \text{ bytes}$$ **Step 4:** Convert to MB: $$b_{\text{MB}} = \frac{6220800}{1048576} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB}$$ > [!TIP] > Notice that the **RGB color image consumes exactly 3× the storage of an equivalent grayscale image**. This is why many computer-vision algorithms convert color images to grayscale as a preprocessing step. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Relationship Between Physical Size and Pixel Dimensions **Problem:** An image has $M \times N$ pixels and is printed at a resolution of $D$ DPI. Derive the physical dimensions in inches and millimetres. **Step 1:** Physical width in inches (1 inch = $D$ pixels, so $N$ pixels = $N/D$ inches): $$W_{\text{inch}} = \frac{N}{D} \text{ inches}$$ **Step 2:** Physical height in inches: $$H_{\text{inch}} = \frac{M}{D} \text{ inches}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to millimetres using $1 \text{ inch} = 25.4 \text{ mm}$: $$W_{\text{mm}} = \frac{N}{D} \times 25.4 \text{ mm}$$ $$H_{\text{mm}} = \frac{M}{D} \times 25.4 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 4:** Worked example: $M = 1024$, $N = 1024$, $D = 300$ DPI. $$W_{\text{inch}} = \frac{1024}{300} \approx 3.41 \text{ inches}$$ $$W_{\text{mm}} = 3.41 \times 25.4 \approx 86.7 \text{ mm} \approx 8.67 \text{ cm}$$ > [!NOTE] > A square $1024 \times 1024$ image printed at 300 DPI is approximately a **8.67 cm × 8.67 cm** print — typical photo-print size. --- ## 3.6 Derivation 4 — Number of Intensity Levels in a Binary Sub-Image **Problem:** A binary image is one where each pixel is either 0 or 1. Prove that a binary image requires exactly 1 bit per pixel. **Step 1:** A binary pixel has only 2 possible values: $\{0, 1\}$. **Step 2:** The number of gray levels: $$L = 2$$ **Step 3:** Solve for the required bits $k$ using $L = 2^k$: $$2 = 2^k \implies k = 1 \text{ bit/pixel}$$ **Step 4:** Storage for an $M \times N$ binary image: $$b_{\text{bits}} = M \times N \times 1 = M \times N \text{ bits}$$ This is why **document-scanning and fingerprint-storage** systems can hold millions of images using minimal memory. --- ## 3.7 Derivation 5 — Dynamic Range in Decibels **Problem:** Convert the dynamic range (a ratio) to decibels (dB), the unit used in image sensors. **Step 1:** Definition of dynamic range as a ratio: $$\text{DR}_{\text{ratio}} = \frac{f_{\max}}{f_{\min}}$$ **Step 2:** In decibels, the dynamic range is defined as: $$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{f_{\max}}{f_{\min}}\right)$$ (The factor 20 comes from the fact that intensity is proportional to amplitude squared.) **Step 3:** Worked example: If $f_{\max} = 255$ and $f_{\min} = 1$ (standard 8-bit image): $$\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(255) = 20 \times 2.4065 \approx 48.13 \text{ dB}$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Note:** Some textbooks (Gonzalez 4th Edition) define dynamic range as the number of bits used to represent the maximum intensity, i.e., $\text{DR} = k$ for a $k$-bit image. Be aware of this convention difference; the most widely accepted definition in DIP is the ratio form. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Image Formation Model — Block Diagram The following block-level functional diagram captures the complete image formation pipeline from physical world to digital representation. Every student should be able to draw or describe this pipeline in 2–3 lines during a viva. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SRC1["ILLUMINATION SOURCE"] S1A["Light Source
Sun Lamp Laser"] end subgraph SRC2["SCENE OBJECTS"] S2A["Physical Object
Reflectance r x y"] end subgraph SRC3["SENSOR"] S3A["Optical Lens
Aperture Filter"] S3B["Sensor Array
CCD or CMOS"] end subgraph SRC4["DIGITIZER"] S4A["Sampler
M by N Grid"] S4B["Quantizer
L Levels"] end subgraph SRC5["DIGITAL IMAGE"] S5A["f x y Matrix
M by N by k bits"] end S1A -- i x y --> S2A S2A -- reflected rays --> S3A S3A --> S3B S3B --> S4A S4A --> S4B S4B --> S5A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Light from the **source** $i(x,y)$ strikes the **scene** with reflectance $r(x,y)$. The product $i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$ is captured by the **sensor array**, then converted to a digital image by the **sampler** (spatial discretization) and **quantizer** (intensity discretization). This is the formal **image formation model** referenced in the KTU 2024 syllabus. --- ## 4.2 Image Representation Hierarchy — Conceptual Map The following flowchart maps the **hierarchical organization** of a digital image from a single pixel up to a complete video sequence. It is a high-density revision aid for Module 1. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["DIGITAL IMAGE f x y"] A0 --> A1["PIXEL
Single Sample f m n"] A0 --> A2["MATRIX
M by N Grid of Pixels"] A0 --> A3["CHANNELS
1 Grayscale or 3 RGB"] A0 --> A4["BIT DEPTH
k bits per pixel"] A1 --> A1A["Smallest Element"] A1 --> A1B["Integer in 0 to L minus 1"] A2 --> A2A["2D Discrete Function"] A2 --> A2B["Origin at Top Left"] A3 --> A3A["Scalar for Grayscale"] A3 --> A3B["3 Vector for RGB"] A4 --> A4A["L equals 2 to power k"] A4 --> A4B["Storage equals M times N times k"] A0 --> A5["FULL COLOR RGB IMAGE"] A0 --> A6["GRAYSCALE IMAGE"] A0 --> A7["BINARY IMAGE"] A0 --> A8["INDEXED IMAGE"] A0 --> A9["VIDEO FRAME SEQUENCE"] A5 --> A5A["24 bits per pixel"] A6 --> A6A["8 bits per pixel typical"] A7 --> A7A["1 bit per pixel"] A8 --> A8A["8 bits index plus palette"] A9 --> A9A["Multiple f x y t frames"] ``` --- ## 4.3 Sampling and Quantization — Functional Flow The following block diagram visualizes the **two-step digitization process** that converts a continuous image into a digital one. Sampling controls the spatial resolution; quantization controls the gray-level resolution. ```mermaid flowchart LR P0["CONTINUOUS IMAGE
f x y continuous"] P0 --> P1["SAMPLING STAGE
Choose M and N"] P1 --> P2["SAMPLED IMAGE
f x y on M by N grid"] P2 --> P3["QUANTIZATION STAGE
Choose L equals 2 to power k"] P3 --> P4["DIGITAL IMAGE
f x y integers"] P1 -.->|higher M N| P1A["Finer spatial detail
less pixelation"] P3 -.->|higher k| P3A["Smoother gray tones
less false contouring"] ``` > [!TIP] > **Viva-ready line:** "Sampling controls *how many* pixels we have; quantization controls *what shades* those pixels can take." Memorize this distinction — it is a 3-mark favourite. --- ## 4.4 Bit Depth vs. Number of Gray Levels — Visual Map ```mermaid flowchart LR Q1["k equals 1 bit"] --> Q1A["L equals 2 levels
Black and White"] Q2["k equals 2 bits"] --> Q2A["L equals 4 levels"] Q3["k equals 4 bits"] --> Q3A["L equals 16 levels"] Q4["k equals 8 bits"] --> Q4A["L equals 256 levels
Standard Grayscale"] Q5["k equals 12 bits"] --> Q5A["L equals 4096 levels
Medical Imaging"] Q6["k equals 16 bits"] --> Q6A["L equals 65536 levels
Scientific"] Q7["k equals 24 bits"] --> Q7A["L equals 16.7M colors
Truecolor RGB"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define a Digital Image. Explain the image formation model. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1, Q1a]` **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks / ~80 words):** A digital image is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ whose spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ and intensity values $f$ are all finite, discrete quantities. It can be represented as an $M \times N$ matrix of pixels. The **image formation model** states that the intensity at any point is the product of two physical components: $$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$ where $i(x, y)$ is the **illumination** incident on the scene ($0 < i(x, y) < \infty$) and $r(x, y)$ is the **reflectance** of the object ($0 < r(x, y) < 1$). $f(x, y) = 0$ is interpreted as **black** and the maximum $f$ as **white**. --- ### Question 2: What is the relationship between the number of gray levels $L$ and the number of bits $k$ per pixel? Mention the values of $L$ for $k = 1, 4, 8$, and $24$. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2, Q1b]` **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks / ~70 words):** The relationship is: $$L = 2^k \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad k = \log_2 L$$ Where $L$ is the total number of distinguishable intensity levels and $k$ is the number of bits used to represent each pixel. | $k$ (bits) | $L = 2^k$ (levels) | Application | |------------|--------------------|-------------| | 1 | 2 | Binary image (B&W) | | 4 | 16 | Low-quality graphics | | 8 | 256 | Standard grayscale | | 24 | 16777216 | Truecolor RGB ($8 \times 3$ channels) | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A: Image Representation, Storage Calculation, and Resolution Analysis `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2a]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Image Representation and Storage **Model Solution:** **(i) Image as a 2D Function (2 marks):** A digital image is a 2D discrete function $f(x, y)$. The image can be represented as a matrix: $$f(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} f(0,0) & f(0,1) & \cdots & f(0,N-1) \\ f(1,0) & f(1,1) & \cdots & f(1,N-1) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ f(M-1,0) & f(M-1,1) & \cdots & f(M-1,N-1) \end{bmatrix}$$ **(ii) Components of $f(x, y)$ (2 marks):** $$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$ where $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$ is the illumination and $0 < r(x, y) < 1$ is the reflectance. **(iii) Storage formula (1 mark):** $$b = M \times N \times k \text{ bits} \quad \text{where } k = \log_2 L$$ **(iv) Worked example: $1024 \times 1024$, 8-bit grayscale (2 marks):** $$b = 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 8388608 \text{ bits} = 1048576 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 1 \text{ MB}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Comparison of Spatial and Gray-Level Resolution **Model Solution:** | Aspect | Spatial Resolution | Gray-Level Resolution | |--------|-------------------|----------------------| | Definition | Smallest discernible spatial detail | Smallest discernible intensity change | | Controlled by | Number of samples $M \times N$ | Number of bits per pixel $k$ | | Effect of increase | Smoother, finer image | Smoother tonal transitions | | Effect of decrease | Visible pixels (blocky) | Visible contour bands (false contouring) | | Unit | DPI, pixels per unit length | Number of gray levels $L = 2^k$ | **Effect of reducing spatial resolution:** Pixelation becomes visible; image looks blocky; diagonal edges become staircase-shaped (jaggies). Reducing $M$ and $N$ by a factor of 2 in each direction reduces the number of pixels by 4× but preserves $k$. **Effect of reducing gray-level resolution:** False contouring appears as bands of constant intensity in smoothly varying regions (e.g., a sky gradient shows discrete bands instead of smooth transition). Reducing $k$ from 8 to 4 gives 16 bands instead of 256; reducing $k$ from 8 to 2 gives only 4 bands, which is unacceptable for photographs. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion:** Both resolutions are critical. In general, an image is acceptable if $M \times N \geq 1024 \times 1024$ and $k \geq 8$ for standard display purposes. --- ### Question B (Internal Choice): RGB Color Model and Digital Image Storage `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q2b - Alternative Choice]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks: RGB Color Model Representation **Model Solution:** In the **RGB color model**, every color visible on a display is generated as an additive combination of three primary components: **Red (R), Green (G), and Blue (B)**. Each pixel is a 3-vector: $$\mathbf{C}(x, y) = \begin{bmatrix} R(x, y) \\ G(x, y) \\ B(x, y) \end{bmatrix}$$ **Properties:** - Each component is an 8-bit unsigned integer in $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$ for a 24-bit color image. - Total bits per pixel: $k = 3 \times 8 = 24$ bits. - Total colors representable: $L = 2^{24} = 16,777,216$ (also called **Truecolor** or **16M colors**). - $(0, 0, 0)$ represents **black** (no light). - $(255, 255, 255)$ represents **white** (all light at maximum). - $(255, 0, 0)$ is pure **red**, $(0, 255, 0)$ is pure **green**, $(0, 0, 255)$ is pure **blue**. **Storage for an $M \times N$ RGB image:** $$b_{\text{RGB}} = M \times N \times 24 \text{ bits} = M \times N \times 3 \text{ bytes}$$ **Worked example (2 marks):** $640 \times 480$ RGB image: $$b = 640 \times 480 \times 24 = 7372800 \text{ bits} = 921600 \text{ bytes} \approx 900 \text{ KB}$$ --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Image as a Matrix and Pixel Indexing **Model Solution:** Consider a $4 \times 4$ grayscale image with values: | $f(x, y)$ | $x=0$ | $x=1$ | $x=2$ | $x=3$ | |-----------|-------|-------|-------|-------| | $y=0$ | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | | $y=1$ | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 | | $y=2$ | 90 | 100 | 110 | 120 | | $y=3$ | 130 | 140 | 150 | 160 | **Observations (3 marks):** 1. The image has $M = 4$ rows and $N = 4$ columns, hence $4 \times 4 = 16$ pixels. 2. $f(0, 0) = 10$ — the top-left pixel; $f(3, 3) = 160$ — the bottom-right pixel. 3. The intensity at any pixel $(x, y)$ can be indexed as $f(y, x)$ where $y$ is the row index and $x$ is the column index. **Storage and bit-depth analysis (2 marks):** - Maximum intensity = 160, which fits in 8 bits (since $160 < 256 = 2^8$). - Therefore $k = 8$ bits/pixel, $L = 256$ levels. - Total storage = $4 \times 4 \times 8 = 128$ bits = 16 bytes. **Linear indexing (MATLAB-style) (2 marks):** Pixels can also be indexed linearly using row-major order: $$p = y \cdot N + x + 1$$ For example, $f(1, 2) = 70$ corresponds to linear index $p = 1 \cdot 4 + 2 + 1 = 7$. This converts the 2D image into a 1D vector for sequential processing. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Marks-Loss Hotspots in Image Representation Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing $L$ and $k$:** Students often write "$L$ is the bit depth" — it is not. $k$ is the bit depth and $L = 2^k$ is the number of gray levels. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this. > 2. **Forgetting unit conversion in storage problems:** A 14-mark question often demands the answer in **MB**, not bits. Always write the explicit chain: bits → bytes (÷8) → KB (÷1024) → MB (÷1024). > 3. **Wrong coordinate convention:** Some students use the mathematical convention (origin at bottom-left) when the question is based on the image-processing convention (origin at top-left). The two conventions are mirror-images of each other and lead to wrong answers in indexing sub-parts. > 4. **Confusing resolution with image size:** "Resolution" is **density per unit area** (DPI), not just the number of pixels. A $1000 \times 1000$ image printed on a 1-inch card has a different resolution than the same image printed on a 10-inch card. > 5. **RGB storage vs grayscale storage:** A common 7-mark sub-part asks "find storage for an RGB image of size $M \times N$ with 8 bits per channel." Students forget the factor of 3 and answer $M \times N \times 8$ instead of the correct $M \times N \times 24$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Digital image** = 2D discrete function $f(x, y)$ that can be represented as an $M \times N$ matrix of pixel intensity values. - **Pixel** = the smallest addressable element of a digital image. - **Image formation model:** $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$, where $i$ is illumination ($0 < i < \infty$) and $r$ is reflectance ($0 < r < 1$). - **Coordinate convention in DIP:** origin at top-left, $x$ increases to the right, $y$ increases downward. - **Bit depth ($k$):** number of bits used to represent the intensity of a single pixel. - **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^k$. Standard grayscale image has $k = 8$, $L = 256$. - **Storage formula:** $b = M \times N \times k$ bits. Convert to bytes by dividing by 8, to KB by dividing by 8192, to MB by dividing by 8388608. - **Spatial resolution** = number of pixels per unit area; controlled by $M \times N$. Unit: DPI. - **Gray-level resolution** = number of distinguishable intensity levels; controlled by $k$. - **Binary image:** $k = 1$, $L = 2$. - **Grayscale image:** typically $k = 8$, $L = 256$. - **Color (RGB) image:** $k = 24$ (three 8-bit channels), $L = 16.7M$ colors. - **Dynamic range:** ratio $f_{\max} / f_{\min}$, also expressed in decibels as $20 \log_{10}(f_{\max}/f_{\min})$. - **Contrast:** difference between object intensity and background intensity. - **Aspect ratio:** $N / M$ (width to height). - **False contouring:** visible banding that appears when $k$ is too low (e.g., $k = 2$ or $k = 4$). - **Pixelation (blockiness):** visible squares that appear when $M$ and $N$ are too low. - **Common storage values to memorize:** $1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 1$ MB (grayscale), $1920 \times 1080 \times 8 \approx 1.99$ MB (HD grayscale), $1920 \times 1080 \times 24 \approx 5.93$ MB (HD RGB). - **Color models to know:** RGB (display), CMY/CMYK (print), HSI/HSV (image processing), YCbCr (video/JPEG). - **Black in digital image:** $f = 0$ for grayscale, $(R, G, B) = (0, 0, 0)$ for RGB. - **White in digital image:** $f = L - 1$ for grayscale, $(R, G, B) = (255, 255, 255)$ for RGB. - **Image as matrix:** $f$ at position $(x, y)$ = value in row $y$, column $x$ (MATLAB convention). - **Linear (row-major) indexing:** $p = y \cdot N + x + 1$ (1-based), or $p = y \cdot N + x$ (0-based). - **DPI to physical size:** $\text{Width in inches} = N / \text{DPI}$; convert to mm by multiplying by 25.4.

topics.title: Types of Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Digital Image?

A **Digital Image** is a two-dimensional discrete function $f(x, y)$ that has been digitized in both **spatial coordinates** ($x, y$) and **intensity (brightness/amplitude)**. The image is composed of a finite number of picture elements called **pixels** (pel = picture element), each carrying a discrete numerical value representing the light intensity at that point.

$$f(x, y) \in \mathbb{Z}, \quad x \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, M-1\}, \quad y \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1\}$$

where $M$ is the number of rows (height) and $N$ is the number of columns (width) of the image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** Module 1 mandates a rigorous understanding of the **classification of digital images based on bit-depth and channel composition** — namely Binary, Grayscale, Color, and Indexed images. This topic is a recurring 3-mark and 14-mark question in university examinations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a giant **mosaic wall** built from millions of tiny colored tiles. Each tile is a **pixel**. Now imagine you have only a few types of tiles:
- If all tiles are either pure **black or pure white**, you get a **Binary Image** (like an old newspaper logo).
- If you have **shades of gray** from black to white, you get a **Grayscale Image** (like a black-and-white photograph).
- If every tile mixes **Red, Green, and Blue** in varying amounts, you get a **Color (RGB) Image** (like your TV screen or smartphone camera output).

The "type" of image is simply determined by **how many possible numerical values** each tile (pixel) can take and **how many color channels** each tile has.

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Classification of Digital Images

Digital images are primarily classified based on:
1. **Bit-depth (intensity resolution):** Number of bits used per pixel.
2. **Channel count (spectral resolution):** Number of color planes stacked together.
3. **Logical meaning (semantic type):** Binary, grayscale, indexed, multispectral, etc.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bit-depth** determines the **range** of intensity values. **Channel count** determines the **dimensionality** of each pixel vector.

---

### 1.4 Definition of Each Major Image Type

| # | Image Type | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Binary Image** | A 1-bit-per-pixel image where each pixel takes only two values: 0 (black) or 1 (white). |
| 2 | **Grayscale Image** | An 8-bit (typically) single-channel image where each pixel represents an intensity value ranging from 0 (black) to 255 (white). |
| 3 | **Color (RGB) Image** | A 24-bit, three-channel image where each pixel is a vector of three intensity values (R, G, B), each ranging 0–255. |
| 4 | **Indexed Image** | A single-channel image whose pixel values are **indices (pointers)** into a separate color lookup table called a colormap/palette. |
| 5 | **Multispectral / Hyperspectral Image** | An image with more than three spectral bands (e.g., satellite imagery with 7–200+ bands). |

---

### 1.5 Why Does "Type of Image" Matter in DIP?

Different image types require **different algorithms**, **different storage**, and **different display mechanisms**. For example:
- Edge detection on a binary image uses **morphological operators** like erosion/dilation.
- Edge detection on a grayscale image uses **gradient filters** like Sobel/Prewitt.
- Compression of RGB images uses **transform coding (DCT/Discrete Cosine Transform)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Insight:** Whenever a question says "explain the types of images," examiners expect a minimum of **Binary, Grayscale, and Color (RGB)** — with formulas for storage and pixel value ranges. Indexed and Multispectral images are bonus marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Binary Image — Deep Analysis

A binary image is the **simplest digital image** in terms of information content. Every pixel belongs to one of exactly **two classes**.

- **Bit-depth:** $k = 1$ bit per pixel.
- **Possible intensity levels:** $L = 2^k = 2^1 = \mathbf{2}$.
- **Pixel value range:** $\{0, 1\}$ where $0$ conventionally represents **black** and $1$ represents **white** (note: MATLAB and some frameworks reverse this, so always verify the convention).
- **Storage requirement:** For an $M \times N$ binary image:

$$\text{Storage bits} = M \times N \times 1 = M \times N \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Storage bytes} = \frac{M \times N}{8}$$

- **Real-world utility:** Document scanning (OCR pre-processing), mask generation, fingerprint recognition, industrial defect detection, morphological operations.

> [!NOTE]
> Binary images are typically produced by **thresholding** a grayscale image: $f_b(x,y) = 1$ if $f(x,y) \geq T$, else $0$.

---

### 2.2 Grayscale Image — Deep Analysis

A grayscale image carries **intensity-only** information with no color. It mimics the human eye's luminance perception.

- **Standard bit-depth:** $k = 8$ bits per pixel (most common in KTU syllabus).
- **Possible intensity levels:** $L = 2^8 = \mathbf{256}$ distinct shades.
- **Pixel value range:** $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$ where $0$ = black, $255$ = white, and intermediate values represent shades of gray.
- **Normalized range:** $[0, 1]$ where intensity $I_{norm} = \dfrac{I_{original}}{255}$.
- **Storage requirement:**

$$\text{Storage bits} = M \times N \times 8 = 8MN \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Storage bytes} = M \times N \text{ bytes}$$

- **Real-world utility:** Medical imaging (X-rays, CT), satellite imagery (single-band), face recognition pre-processing, edge detection, histogram equalization.

- **Why 8-bit and not more?** The human eye can distinguish only about **128–256 shades of gray** under typical viewing conditions. Hence 8 bits is the engineering sweet spot between fidelity and storage.

---

### 2.3 Color (RGB) Image — Deep Analysis

A true-color RGB image is essentially **three grayscale images stacked together**, one each for the **Red, Green, and Blue** primary channels.

- **Bit-depth:** $k = 24$ bits per pixel (8 bits per channel).
- **Possible color levels per channel:** $2^8 = 256$.
- **Total possible colors:** $2^{24} = \mathbf{16,777,216} \approx 16.7 \text{ million}$ (often called "True Color").
- **Pixel value range per channel:** $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$.
- **Storage requirement:**

$$\text{Storage bits} = M \times N \times 3 \times 8 = 24MN \text{ bits} = 3MN \text{ bytes}$$

- **Real-world utility:** Digital photography, video streaming, display technology (monitors, smartphones), computer vision.

- **Additive Color Model:** RGB is **additive** — combining all three at maximum (255, 255, 255) gives **white**, and (0, 0, 0) gives **black**.

---

### 2.4 Indexed Image — Deep Analysis

An indexed image is a **memory-optimized** representation of color. Instead of storing three channels, it stores a **single index** per pixel that points into a shared **Color Look-Up Table (CLUT)**.

- **Pixel value:** Integer index in the range $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, L-1\}$.
- **CLUT/Colormap dimensions:** $L \times 3$ (one RGB triple per index).
- **Bit-depth:** Depends on palette size. For $L = 256$ colors, $k = 8$ bits per pixel (same as grayscale in size, but with color!).
- **Total possible distinct colors:** Limited to palette size $L$.
- **Storage requirement:**

$$\text{Storage bits} = (M \times N \times k) + (L \times 24) \text{ bits}$$

- **Real-world utility:** GIF image format, older indexed PNGs, embedded systems with limited RAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Point:** Indexed images are **NOT** grayscale images. Two images with the same bit-depth (e.g., 8-bit) can either be grayscale (intensity directly) or indexed (pointer to a palette). This is a frequent exam trick question.

---

### 2.5 Multispectral / Hyperspectral Image — Deep Analysis

These images extend beyond the visible RGB spectrum into infrared, ultraviolet, or thermal bands.

- **Number of bands:** $B \geq 4$ (multispectral) or $B > 100$ (hyperspectral).
- **Bit-depth:** Typically 11–16 bits per pixel per band (to capture subtle spectral variations).
- **Storage requirement:**

$$\text{Storage bits} = M \times N \times B \times k$$

- **Real-world utility:** Remote sensing (LANDSAT, Sentinel), astronomy, agricultural monitoring, mineral exploration.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU DIP exam.**

| Parameter | Binary | Grayscale (8-bit) | RGB (24-bit) | Indexed (L colors) |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| Bits per pixel ($k$) | $1$ | $8$ | $24$ | $\log_2 L$ |
| Levels ($L = 2^k$) | $2$ | $256$ | $16{,}777{,}216$ | $L$ |
| Pixel range | $\{0, 1\}$ | $[0, 255]$ | $[0, 255]$ per channel | $[0, L-1]$ |
| Storage (bits) | $MN$ | $8MN$ | $24MN$ | $MN \log_2 L + 24L$ |
| Storage (bytes) | $MN/8$ | $MN$ | $3MN$ | $\dfrac{MN \log_2 L}{8} + 3L$ |
| Number of channels | $1$ | $1$ | $3$ | $1$ + CLUT |

### 2.7 General Storage Formula (Master Equation)

For **any** digital image of size $M \times N$ with $B$ bands and $k$ bits per pixel per band:

$$\boxed{\;\text{Storage size (bits)} = M \times N \times B \times k\;}$$

$$\text{Storage size (bytes)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times B \times k}{8}$$

$$\text{Storage size (KB)} = \dfrac{M \times N \times B \times k}{8 \times 1024}$$

### 2.8 Number of Gray Levels Formula

$$\boxed{\;L = 2^k \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad k = \log_2 L\;}$$

This is **bidirectional**: if a question asks "how many bits are needed for 1024 gray levels?" the answer is $k = \log_2 1024 = 10$ bits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Storage Required for an Image of Dimension $M \times N$ with $k$ Bits/Pixel

**Statement:** Prove that an image of size $M \times N$ with $k$ bits per pixel requires exactly $M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits of storage.

**Proof (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Count the total number of pixels.**
Every image is a rectangular matrix of pixels. Each row contains $N$ pixels (columns), and there are $M$ such rows.

$$\text{Total pixels} = (\text{number of rows}) \times (\text{number of columns per row}) = M \times N$$

**Step 2 — Bits consumed by a single pixel.**
By the definition of bit-depth, each pixel stores its intensity value using exactly $k$ bits.

$$\text{Bits per pixel} = k$$

**Step 3 — Multiply to get total bits.**
Since each pixel independently stores $k$ bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total storage (bits)} &= (\text{Total pixels}) \times (\text{Bits per pixel}) \\
&= (M \times N) \times k \\
&= M \cdot N \cdot k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to higher units.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total storage (bytes)} &= \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8} \\
\text{Total storage (KB)} &= \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8 \times 1024} = \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8192} \\
\text{Total storage (MB)} &= \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8 \times 1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8{,}388{,}608}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hence proved.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example #1: Grayscale Image Storage

**Problem:** Find the storage required (in KB) for a **grayscale image** of size $1024 \times 768$ pixels with 8 bits/pixel.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify given values.
- $M = 1024$ pixels (rows)
- $N = 768$ pixels (columns)
- $k = 8$ bits/pixel (grayscale standard)
- $B = 1$ band (grayscale is single-channel)

**Step 2:** Apply the storage formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage (bits)} &= M \times N \times B \times k \\
&= 1024 \times 768 \times 1 \times 8 \\
&= 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert bits to KB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage (bytes)} &= \dfrac{6{,}291{,}456}{8} = 786{,}432 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Storage (KB)} &= \dfrac{786{,}432}{1024} = 768 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The image requires exactly **768 KB** of storage.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** A common trick is to ask for an RGB image of the same size — the answer would simply be **3 × 768 KB = 2304 KB = 2.25 MB** since there are 3 channels.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example #2: Bit-Depth from Gray Levels

**Problem:** A medical imaging system produces images with **4096 distinct gray levels**. How many bits per pixel are required?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** We are given $L = 4096$ distinct intensity levels.

**Step 2:** Use the bidirectional formula $L = 2^k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
4096 &= 2^k \\
2^{12} &= 2^k \\
k &= 12 \text{ bits/pixel}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Verification.

$$2^{12} = 4096 \checkmark$$

**Answer:** The system requires **12 bits per pixel**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world context:** Medical images (CT, MRI) typically use **12-bit** or **16-bit** depth because subtle tissue differences (e.g., a tumor vs. healthy tissue) must be captured with high precision.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example #3: RGB Image Storage Calculation

**Problem:** A smartphone captures an RGB photograph of size $4000 \times 3000$ (12 megapixels). Calculate the uncompressed storage in MB.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters.
- $M = 4000$ pixels
- $N = 3000$ pixels
- $B = 3$ bands (RGB)
- $k = 8$ bits/pixel per band
- Total bits per pixel = $3 \times 8 = 24$ bits

**Step 2:** Apply the storage formula.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage (bits)} &= 4000 \times 3000 \times 3 \times 8 \\
&= 288{,}000{,}000 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to MB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage (bytes)} &= \dfrac{288{,}000{,}000}{8} = 36{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes} \\
\text{Storage (MB)} &= \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{36{,}000{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 34.33 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The uncompressed RGB photograph requires approximately **34.33 MB**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why JPEG compression matters:** A 12 MP raw image is ~34 MB, but a JPEG-compressed version is typically 3–6 MB — that's a **~85% reduction** with minimal perceptual loss. This motivates the study of image compression in later modules.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example #4: Indexed Image Trade-off

**Problem:** Compare the storage of a $512 \times 512$ image stored as (a) 24-bit RGB, and (b) indexed with a 256-color palette.

**Solution (a) — RGB storage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Storage (bits)} &= 512 \times 512 \times 3 \times 8 = 6{,}291{,}456 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Storage (bytes)} &= \dfrac{6{,}291{,}456}{8} = 786{,}432 \text{ bytes} \approx 768 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution (b) — Indexed image storage:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Image data (bits)} &= 512 \times 512 \times 8 = 2{,}097{,}152 \text{ bits} \\
\text{CLUT overhead (bits)} &= 256 \times 24 = 6{,}144 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total storage (bits)} &= 2{,}097{,}152 + 6{,}144 = 2{,}103{,}296 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total storage (bytes)} &= \dfrac{2{,}103{,}296}{8} = 262{,}912 \text{ bytes} \approx 256.75 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 768 - 256.75 = 511.25 \text{ KB} \approx 66.6\% \text{ reduction}
$$

**Answer:** Indexed storage saves **~66.6%** of the memory at the cost of limiting the color palette to 256 distinct colors.

---

### 3.6 Master Summary — Quick Computation Table

| Scenario | $M \times N$ | $B$ | $k$ | Bits | Bytes | KB |
|----------|--------------|-----|-----|------|-------|----|
| Binary icon | $64 \times 64$ | $1$ | $1$ | $4096$ | $512$ | $0.5$ |
| Grayscale photo | $1024 \times 768$ | $1$ | $8$ | $6.29$ M | $786{,}432$ | $768$ |
| RGB photo (HD) | $1920 \times 1080$ | $3$ | $8$ | $49.8$ M | $6.22$ M | $6{,}075$ |
| RGB photo (12 MP) | $4000 \times 3000$ | $3$ | $8$ | $288$ M | $36$ M | $35{,}156$ |
| Medical (12-bit) | $512 \times 512$ | $1$ | $12$ | $3.15$ M | $393{,}216$ | $384$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Digital Images (Mermaid Tree)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["DIGITAL IMAGES"] --> B["Binary 1 bit per pixel"]
    A --> C["Grayscale 8 bit single channel"]
    A --> D["Color RGB 24 bit three channel"]
    A --> E["Indexed 8 bit with CLUT palette"]
    A --> F["Multispectral B greater than 3 bands"]

    B --> B1["Values 0 or 1"]
    B --> B2["Use Mask OCR Morphology"]

    C --> C1["Values 0 to 255"]
    C --> C2["Use Edge Detection Histogram"]

    D --> D1["Three stacked planes R G B"]
    D --> D2["Use Compression Color Spaces HSV YCbCr"]

    E --> E1["Index plus Colormap"]
    E --> E2["Use GIF PNG8 Legacy Graphics"]

    F --> F1["Bands 4 to 200 plus"]
    F --> F2["Use Remote Sensing Astronomy"]
```

---

### 4.2 Pixel Representation Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Image Source Camera Scanner Sensor"] --> s1["Sampling Spatial Digitization"]
    s1 --> s2["Quantization Intensity Digitization"]
    s2 --> p1["Pixel at x y"]
    p1 --> q1["How many bits per pixel k"]
    q1 --> r1["Bit depth equals 1"]
    q1 --> r2["Bit depth equals 8"]
    q1 --> r3["Bit depth equals 24"]
    q1 --> r4["Bit depth equals k generic"]

    r1 --> out1["Binary Image Two levels"]
    r2 --> out2["Grayscale Image 256 levels"]
    r3 --> out3["RGB Image 16.7 million colors"]
    r4 --> out4["Specialized k bit Image"]
```

---

### 4.3 Channel Stacking Visualization (RGB Image)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph P["Single Pixel as a Vector Triple"]
        R["Red Plane value 0 to 255"]
        G["Green Plane value 0 to 255"]
        Bch["Blue Plane value 0 to 255"]
    end

    R --> merge["Stacked RGB Vector 24 bits total"]
    G --> merge
    Bch --> merge

    merge --> out["Final Color perceived by viewer"]

    out --> w1["Example 255 255 255 equals White"]
    out --> w2["Example 0 0 0 equals Black"]
    out --> w3["Example 255 0 0 equals Pure Red"]
    out --> w4["Example 0 255 0 equals Pure Green"]
```

---

### 4.4 Indexed Image Architecture (Lookup Mechanism)

```mermaid
graph LR
    p["Pixel value equals Index 0 to L minus 1"] --> lookup["Pointer arrow"]
    lookup --> cmap["Colormap CLUT of size L by 3"]
    cmap --> r["Red component"]
    cmap --> g["Green component"]
    cmap --> b["Blue component"]
    r --> final["Final displayed color"]
    g --> final
    b --> final
```

---

### 4.5 Bit-Depth vs Visual Quality Comparison Matrix

| Bit Depth | Levels | Visual Example Use Case | Storage per Pixel |
|-----------|--------|-------------------------|-------------------|
| 1 bit | 2 | Text documents, fax | 1 bit |
| 2 bit | 4 | Vintage displays | 2 bits |
| 4 bit | 16 | Old gaming consoles | 4 bits |
| 8 bit | 256 | Standard grayscale, GIF | 8 bits = 1 byte |
| 16 bit | 65,536 | Medical imaging, RAW photography | 2 bytes |
| 24 bit | 16.7 M | True color RGB | 3 bytes |
| 32 bit | 4.29 B | RGB plus alpha transparency | 4 bytes |
| 48 bit | 281 T | Professional RAW RGB | 6 bytes |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define the following with respect to digital image types:**
(a) Binary image
(b) Grayscale image
(c) RGB image

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Binary Image:** A digital image in which each pixel can take only **two possible intensity values**, conventionally $0$ (black) and $1$ (white). It uses **1 bit per pixel** and is also called a **bi-level** or **monochrome** image.

**(b) Grayscale Image:** A single-channel digital image in which each pixel represents an **intensity value** between **black and white**. The standard grayscale image uses **8 bits per pixel**, giving $2^8 = 256$ distinct shades ranging from $0$ (black) to $255$ (white).

**(c) RGB Image:** A true-color image represented as a combination of three independent intensity planes — **Red, Green, and Blue** — each typically 8 bits deep. Total bit-depth is **24 bits/pixel**, allowing $2^{24} \approx 16.77$ million distinct colors. The RGB model is **additive**: $(0,0,0)$ produces black and $(255,255,255)$ produces white.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Distinguish between a grayscale image and an indexed image. Mention the role of a colormap.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Grayscale Image | Indexed Image |
|----------|-----------------|---------------|
| Bit-depth | 8 bits/pixel (typical) | 8 bits/pixel (typical) |
| Pixel value meaning | Direct intensity (0 = black, 255 = white) | Index/pointer into a separate color table |
| Color support | No color, only shades of gray | Supports up to $2^k$ colors (via colormap) |
| Additional data | None | Requires a **Colormap (CLUT)** of size $L \times 3$ |
| Storage | $M \times N$ bytes | $M \times N + 3L$ bytes |
| Example use | X-rays, edge maps | GIF, PNG-8, palette graphics |

**Role of Colormap:** The colormap (also called **CLUT — Color Look-Up Table** or **palette**) is a separate matrix of dimensions $L \times 3$ that stores the actual RGB color values. Each pixel's value acts as a **row index** into this table, retrieving its displayed color. Without the colormap, an indexed image cannot be rendered correctly.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the different types of digital images based on bit-depth. For each type, state the bit-depth, number of gray levels, and a typical application.**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A grayscale image of size $2560 \times 1440$ pixels uses 8 bits per pixel. Compute the storage required in (i) bits, (ii) bytes, (iii) kilobytes, and (iv) megabytes. If the same image were stored as an RGB image, what would be the new storage in MB?**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

The classification of digital images based on bit-depth is as follows:

**1. Binary Image (1-bit image):**
- **Bit-depth:** $k = 1$ bit/pixel.
- **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^1 = 2$.
- **Pixel values:** $\{0, 1\}$ representing black and white.
- **Application:** Document scanning, optical character recognition (OCR), printed circuit board inspection, morphological operations.

**2. Grayscale Image (8-bit image):**
- **Bit-depth:** $k = 8$ bits/pixel.
- **Number of gray levels:** $L = 2^8 = 256$.
- **Pixel values:** $\{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\}$.
- **Application:** Medical X-ray imaging, satellite single-band imagery, face detection pre-processing, edge detection.

**3. RGB Color Image (24-bit image):**
- **Bit-depth:** $k = 24$ bits/pixel (8 bits per channel × 3 channels).
- **Number of distinct colors:** $L = 2^{24} = 16,777,216$.
- **Pixel values:** Each pixel is a triple $(R, G, B)$ with each component in $\{0, \dots, 255\}$.
- **Application:** Digital photography, video streaming, display screens.

**4. Indexed Image (palette-based):**
- **Bit-depth:** Typically $k = 8$ bits/pixel (allows 256 colors).
- **Number of colors:** Limited by palette size, e.g., 256.
- **Pixel values:** Integers $\{0, \dots, L-1\}$ used as indices into a **colormap**.
- **Application:** GIF graphics, web icons, embedded systems.

**5. Multispectral Image:**
- **Bit-depth:** Often 11–16 bits per band.
- **Number of bands:** $B \geq 4$ (e.g., 7 for LANDSAT).
- **Application:** Remote sensing, mineral exploration.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Image size: $M = 2560$, $N = 1440$ pixels
- Bit-depth: $k = 8$ bits/pixel (grayscale)
- Bands: $B = 1$ (grayscale)

**Step 1 — Compute total pixels.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total pixels} &= M \times N = 2560 \times 1440 \\
&= 3{,}686{,}400 \text{ pixels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute storage in bits.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bits} &= M \times N \times B \times k \\
&= 3{,}686{,}400 \times 1 \times 8 \\
&= 29{,}491{,}200 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
\text{Bytes} = \dfrac{29{,}491{,}200}{8} = 3{,}686{,}400 \text{ bytes}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kilobytes.**

$$
\text{KB} = \dfrac{3{,}686{,}400}{1024} = 3600 \text{ KB}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to megabytes.**

$$
\text{MB} = \dfrac{3{,}686{,}400}{1024 \times 1024} = \dfrac{3{,}686{,}400}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 3.516 \text{ MB}
$$

**Step 6 — RGB version.**

For an RGB image, $B = 3$, so the storage becomes **3 times** the grayscale storage:

$$
\text{Storage (RGB)} = 3 \times 3.516 \approx 10.547 \text{ MB}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Grayscale: 29,491,200 bits = 3,686,400 bytes = 3600 KB ≈ **3.516 MB**
- RGB: ≈ **10.547 MB**

---

## Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the structure of an RGB color image. Derive the total number of distinct colors it can represent.**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**An indexed image uses a colormap of 64 colors and is of dimension $320 \times 240$. Calculate the total storage (in bytes) required, including the colormap overhead. If this image were converted to grayscale, how much additional memory would be saved?**

---

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Structure of an RGB Image:**

An RGB image is a **three-dimensional discrete array** of dimensions $M \times N \times 3$. Conceptually, it consists of **three independent grayscale planes** of size $M \times N$, stacked along a third (channel) axis:

- **Red plane** $f_R(x, y)$: contains red component intensity at each pixel.
- **Green plane** $f_G(x, y)$: contains green component intensity at each pixel.
- **Blue plane** $f_B(x, y)$: contains blue component intensity at each pixel.

When displayed, these three planes are **superimposed** additively, and the human visual system perceives a single full-color image.

Each plane carries 8 bits per pixel, so each pixel is a triple:

$$f(x, y) = \left[ f_R(x, y), \; f_G(x, y), \; f_B(x, y) \right], \quad \text{where each } f_i \in [0, 255]$$

**Derivation of Total Distinct Colors:**

**Step 1:** Each of the three channels has $2^8 = 256$ possible intensity values.

**Step 2:** The total number of distinct color combinations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total colors} &= 2^8 \times 2^8 \times 2^8 \\
&= 256 \times 256 \times 256 \\
&= 2^{8+8+8} \\
&= 2^{24}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $2^{24}$.

$$
2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216
$$

**Answer:** An RGB image can represent a maximum of **16,777,216 (~16.77 million)** distinct colors, commonly known as **True Color**.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- Image dimension: $M = 320$, $N = 240$ pixels.
- Colormap size: $L = 64$ colors.

**Step 1 — Compute bits per index.**

For $L = 64$ distinct indices, the required bit-depth is:

$$
k = \log_2 64 = 6 \text{ bits/pixel}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute image data storage (bits).**

$$
\text{Image data (bits)} = M \times N \times k = 320 \times 240 \times 6 = 460{,}800 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute colormap overhead (bits).**

Each colormap entry stores 3 bytes (24 bits) of RGB color. With $L = 64$ entries:

$$
\text{CLUT (bits)} = 64 \times 24 = 1536 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Total indexed storage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total (bits)} &= 460{,}800 + 1536 = 462{,}336 \text{ bits} \\
\text{Total (bytes)} &= \dfrac{462{,}336}{8} = 57{,}792 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Grayscale equivalent storage.**

An 8-bit grayscale image of the same size requires:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Grayscale (bytes)} &= M \times N \times 1 \\
&= 320 \times 240 = 76{,}800 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Memory savings (or cost).**

$$
\Delta = 76{,}800 - 57{,}792 = 19{,}008 \text{ bytes saved by using indexed}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- Indexed storage (with CLUT): **57,792 bytes ≈ 56.44 KB**
- Grayscale storage: **76,800 bytes = 75 KB**
- Memory saved: **19,008 bytes ≈ 18.56 KB**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the colormap overhead** in indexed image problems. Students often compute only $M \times N \times k$ and miss the $3L$-byte CLUT overhead. Examiners deduct **2 marks** for this.
>
> 2. **Confusing $k = 8$ with grayscale.** When an image is *indexed*, the 8-bit pixel value is an **index**, not a direct intensity. Marking this as "grayscale" loses **3 marks**.
>
> 3. **Wrong unit conversion.** Using $1000$ instead of $1024$ in KB/MB conversions. In DIP, **always use 1024** ($2^{10}$) since it's binary-based. This mistake loses **2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Mixing up RGB channels.** Writing $24$ bits-per-pixel-total as $8$ bits for one channel — the 8-bit depth is **per channel**, not total. Examiners expect explicit mention of "8 bits per channel, 24 bits per pixel."
>
> 5. **Skipping the formula derivation.** Direct substitution without stating $L = 2^k$ loses **1–2 marks**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Read this before entering the exam hall:**

- **Binary image:** $k = 1$, $L = 2$, pixel range $\{0, 1\}$. Used for masks, OCR, morphology.
- **Grayscale image:** $k = 8$, $L = 256$, pixel range $[0, 255]$. Most common DIP input.
- **RGB image:** $k = 24$ (8 per channel), $L = 2^{24} = 16{,}777{,}216$ colors. Three stacked planes.
- **Indexed image:** Pixel value is a **pointer** into a colormap, not direct intensity. Requires CLUT overhead.
- **Multispectral image:** $B > 3$ bands; used in remote sensing and medical imaging.
- **Master storage formula:** $\text{Size (bits)} = M \times N \times B \times k$.
- **Master gray-level formula:** $L = 2^k \Longleftrightarrow k = \log_2 L$.
- **Unit conversions:** $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$, $1 \text{ KB} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$, $1 \text{ MB} = 1024 \text{ KB}$.
- **True-color RGB** = $16.77$ million colors; **Human eye grayscale perception** ≈ 256 shades.
- **Pure white in RGB** = $(255, 255, 255)$; **Pure black** = $(0, 0, 0)$; **Pure red** = $(255, 0, 0)$.
- **Examiner favorite questions:** "Compute storage for $M \times N$ grayscale/RGB image" and "Find bits per pixel for $L$ gray levels" — practice both at least three times.
- **Indexed vs. Grayscale trap:** Same bit-depth (e.g., 8-bit), but **different semantic meaning** — don't confuse in answers.
- **Always state assumptions** when computing storage: whether $B = 1$ or $3$, whether colormap overhead is included, and whether conversion uses 1000 or 1024.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements of DIP System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Elements of DIP System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital Image Processing (DIP) System** is an integrated hardware–software framework that captures real-world visual information through optical sensors, converts the continuous analog signal into a finite digital representation through sampling and quantization, and applies computational algorithms to enhance, analyze, store, or transmit the resulting image data.

Mathematically, a two-dimensional digital image is defined as a discrete function $f(x, y)$ sampled over a finite two-dimensional grid, where the spatial coordinates $(x, y)$ and the intensity value $f$ are all finite, discrete quantities.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Every student must clearly distinguish between the *physical acquisition chain* (sensor + optics + digitizer) and the *computational processing chain* (computer + algorithms + display). Board questions frequently test the ability to label a block diagram end-to-end.

The fundamental purpose of any DIP system is to convert an **analog visual reality** into a **discrete numerical matrix** that a digital computer can manipulate. This involves three non-negotiable stages:

1. **Image Acquisition** — Optical sensing of energy reflected/emitted by physical objects.
2. **Digitization** — Sampling (spatial discretization) and Quantization (intensity discretization).
3. **Image Manipulation and Output** — Storage, processing, display, and transmission.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Digital Camera Pipeline"

Imagine a **traditional film camera** vs. a **smartphone camera** capturing the same sunset.

- The **film in the old camera** is an analog storage medium — every grain of silver halide holds continuous chemical information. It cannot be edited by a computer, cannot be e-mailed, and degrades over time.
- The **smartphone camera**, by contrast, is essentially a miniature DIP system:
  - The **lens** focuses the light (analog stage).
  - The **CMOS/CCD sensor** converts photons into electrical charges.
  - The **Image Signal Processor (ISP)** digitizes these charges into a matrix of numbers — pixels.
  - The **microprocessor and software** apply noise reduction, color correction, and compression.
  - The **OLED display** reconstructs the digital matrix back into visible light.
  - The **cloud network** transmits the image to another device.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Takeaway:** A DIP system is essentially a *translation engine* between the continuous physical world (light, color, depth) and the discrete mathematical world (matrices, integers, algorithms). Every component exists to bridge this gap in one specific direction.

## 1.3 Fundamental Building Blocks (Overview)

| Block | Primary Function | Example Hardware |
|---|---|---|
| Image Sensor | Convert light energy to electrical signal | CCD, CMOS, Photodiode |
| Digitizer | Sample and quantize the analog signal | A/D Converter card |
| Computer | Execute algorithms and control flow | CPU, GPU, DSP |
| Mass Storage | Retain image data (short/long term) | RAM, SSD, Magnetic Disk |
| Display | Reconstruct image as visible form | LCD, LED, Printer |
| Communication | Transmit image between systems | LAN, WAN, Internet |
| Software | Provide algorithmic capability | MATLAB, OpenCV, ImageJ |

These blocks together form the **fundamental architecture** that is universally accepted in KTU 2024 reference materials for Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component-Wise Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Image Acquisition Subsystem

This is the *front-end* of any DIP system and consists of two sub-components working in series:

- **Optical Front-End (Imaging Lens System):** Focuses the energy from the scene onto the sensor plane. The lens obeys classical thin-lens geometry:
$$\frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{d_o} + \frac{1}{d_i}$$
where $f$ is focal length, $d_o$ is object distance, and $d_i$ is image distance. A larger aperture (smaller $f$-number) admits more light, improving low-light performance but reducing depth of field.

- **Image Sensor:** The transducer that converts incident photons into a proportional electrical signal. Two dominant technologies exist:
  - **CCD (Charge Coupled Device):** Charges are shifted bucket-brigade style from pixel to pixel to a common output node. High uniformity, low noise, higher power consumption, slower readout.
  - **CMOS (Active Pixel Sensor):** Each pixel has its own amplifier and row-select transistor. Lower power, faster readout, on-chip integration of control logic, higher noise than CCD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Modern smartphones exclusively use **Back-Side Illuminated (BSI) CMOS** sensors because they integrate well with the rest of the System-on-Chip (SoC), enabling real-time HDR, autofocus pixel-binning, and computational photography pipelines.

### 2.1.2 Digitizer (Sampling + Quantization Block)

The analog electrical signal from the sensor must be discretized in two independent dimensions:

- **Sampling** — Discretizes the *spatial domain* $(x, y)$. Governed by the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
The sampling rate must be at least twice the highest spatial frequency present in the continuous image; otherwise, *aliasing* (Moiré patterns, jagged edges) occurs.

- **Quantization** — Discretizes the *intensity domain* $f$. If the digitizer produces $k$ bits per sample, the total number of distinguishable gray levels is:
$$L = 2^k$$
For a true 8-bit grayscale image, $L = 256$ gray levels. For a 24-bit color image (RGB), $L = 16,777,216$ reproducible colors.

### 2.1.3 Image Processing Hardware

- **General-Purpose CPU:** Sequential control, OS management, low-level image I/O.
- **GPU (Graphics Processing Unit):** Massively parallel SIMD architecture ideal for convolution, FFT, and matrix operations.
- **DSP (Digital Signal Processor):** Specialized for streaming image pipelines in embedded/medical contexts.
- **FPGA / ASIC:** Hard-wired logic for real-time industrial vision, automotive ADAS, and surveillance.

### 2.1.4 Computer and Software Stack

The computer hosts the operating system and specialized image processing libraries. In a KTU 2024 context, students should be familiar with conceptual software architecture: a *kernel* performs pixel-level operations; *middle-layer libraries* perform filtering, segmentation, and morphological operations; an *application layer* performs domain-specific analysis (medical, satellite, biometric).

### 2.1.5 Mass Storage Hierarchy

Storage in a DIP system is tiered based on access speed and capacity:

- **Tier 1 — Volatile (Cache, RAM):** Microsecond access, used for active processing.
- **Tier 2 — Online (SSD, HDD):** Millisecond access, used for project image databases.
- **Tier 3 — Archival (Magnetic Tape, Optical Disc, Cloud):** Seconds-to-minutes access, used for legal/medical archives.

A grayscale image of size $M \times N$ pixels with $k$ bits per pixel requires storage:
$$B = M \times N \times k \text{ bits}$$

### 2.1.6 Image Display Subsystem

- **Soft Display:** CRT/LCD/LED monitors reconstruct a 2-D matrix as a raster of light-emitting elements.
- **Hard Display:** Printers, plotters, and film recorders provide permanent physical output.
- **3D/Holographic Displays:** Advanced systems used in scientific visualization and medical imaging.

### 2.1.7 Communication Subsystem

Image data is voluminous (a single $4096 \times 4096$ 24-bit color image is $\approx 48$ MB uncompressed). The communication block provides:
- **LAN/WAN connectivity** for telemedicine and remote sensing.
- **Standardized compression protocols** (JPEG, JPEG2000, PNG, DICOM) to reduce bandwidth.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of gray levels | $L = 2^k$ | $k$ = bits per pixel, dimensionless |
| 2 | Image storage (bits) | $B = M \times N \times k$ | Bits, $M$ = rows, $N$ = columns |
| 3 | Image storage (bytes) | $B_{bytes} = M \cdot N \cdot k / 8$ | Bytes |
| 4 | Color image storage | $B_{color} = M \cdot N \cdot 3k$ | For 24-bit RGB, $k = 8$ |
| 5 | Number of intensity levels (NEP contrast) | $L = 2^k$ | Same as #1, often re-derived |
| 6 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz, prevents aliasing |
| 7 | Sampling resolution (dpi) | $DPI = \vert\text{pixels per inch}\vert$ | Pixels/inch |
| 8 | Aspect ratio | $AR = M \div N$ | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Euclidean distance | $D_e(p,q) = \sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$ | Standard $\mathbb{R}^2$ norm |
| 10 | City-block distance | $D_4(p,q) = \vert x-s \vert + \vert y-t \vert$ | Manhattan distance |
| 11 | Chessboard distance | $D_8(p,q) = \max(\vert x-s \vert, \vert y-t \vert)$ | Chebyshev distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Examiners frequently award 1 mark for *correctly stating* the unit and 1 mark for *numerical substitution* in any memory/storage computation. Always write the unit explicitly to avoid losing easy marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Elements of a DIP System are the foundational vocabulary of *every* applied imaging discipline:

- **Medical Imaging (MRI, CT, X-ray):** PACS systems (Picture Archiving and Communication Systems) are direct industrial implementations of the same seven-block architecture.
- **Remote Sensing (ISRO Bhuvan, Landsat):** Satellite-borne push-broom CCD sensors + onboard DSP + ground station storage constitute a DIP system deployed at 700 km altitude.
- **Automotive ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Multi-camera CMOS + FPGA + GPU processing + LCD display with CAN-bus communication.
- **Industrial Quality Inspection:** Line-scan CMOS + ASIC + PLC + defect display, running at line speeds exceeding 1000 parts/minute.

A graduate engineer working in any of these domains must be able to *trace the data flow* through the seven blocks fluently.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Storage Requirement of a Grayscale Image

**Statement:** A grayscale image has $M$ rows, $N$ columns, and $k$ bits allocated per pixel. Derive the total storage requirement and express it in bytes, kilobytes, and megabytes.

**Step 1 — Identify the discrete image structure.**
A digital image $f(x, y)$ is sampled on a discrete grid:
$$x \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, M-1\}, \quad y \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1\}$$
The total number of pixel locations is:
$$\text{Total Pixels} = M \times N$$

**Step 2 — Bits per pixel.**
Each pixel stores one of $L = 2^k$ possible intensity values. The bit-width per pixel is exactly $k$ bits.

**Step 3 — Total bit count.**
Multiply the number of pixels by the bits per pixel:
$$B_{bits} = M \cdot N \cdot k$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes.**
Since $1 \text{ byte} = 8 \text{ bits}$:
$$B_{bytes} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to kilobytes / megabytes.**
$$B_{KB} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8 \cdot 1024}, \quad B_{MB} = \frac{M \cdot N \cdot k}{8 \cdot 1024^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $1024$ and not $1000$?** Computers use binary addressing, so $1 \text{ KB} = 2^{10} \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ bytes}$. The SI (decimal) interpretation gives $1 \text{ kB} = 1000 \text{ bytes}$ and is used by storage manufacturers — always specify which you are using.

**Worked Numerical Example:**
A $1024 \times 1024$ image with $k = 8$ bits/pixel:
$$B_{bits} = 1024 \cdot 1024 \cdot 8 = 8,388,608 \text{ bits} = 1,048,576 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ KB} = 1 \text{ MB}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Storage Requirement of an RGB Color Image

**Statement:** A color image uses the RGB color model with 3 channels, each of $k$ bits. Derive the storage formula.

**Step 1 — Recognize three independent channels.**
A color image is a vector-valued function:
$$\mathbf{f}(x,y) = \begin{bmatrix} f_R(x,y) \\ f_G(x,y) \\ f_B(x,y) \end{bmatrix}$$
Each channel is itself a grayscale image.

**Step 2 — Bits per pixel for color.**
Bits per pixel = (Number of channels) × (Bits per channel) = $3k$.
For standard 24-bit true color, $k = 8$, giving $3 \times 8 = 24$ bits per pixel.

**Step 3 — Total storage.**
$$B_{color} = M \cdot N \cdot 3k$$

**Numerical Verification:**
For a $1920 \times 1080$ (Full HD) 24-bit color image:
$$B_{color} = 1920 \cdot 1080 \cdot 24 = 49,766,400 \text{ bits} \approx 5.93 \text{ MB uncompressed}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Distinguishable Gray Levels

**Statement:** Given $k$ quantization bits, derive the maximum number of distinguishable gray levels.

**Step 1 — Each pixel can occupy one of $2^k$ binary codewords.**
$$L = 2^k$$

**Step 2 — Quantization step size.**
If the continuous intensity range is $[0, I_{max}]$, then:
$$\Delta I = \frac{I_{max}}{L - 1} = \frac{I_{max}}{2^k - 1}$$

**Worked Example:**
For an 8-bit system with $I_{max} = 255$:
$$L = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}, \quad \Delta I = \frac{255}{255} = 1 \text{ intensity unit per step}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Neighbors and Distance Measures in a Digital Image

**Statement:** For a pixel $p$ at coordinates $(x, y)$, derive the 4-neighbor, diagonal-neighbor, and 8-neighbor sets, and the three standard distance metrics.

**Step 1 — 4-Neighbors (Manhattan-adjacent pixels).**
$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y), (x-1, y), (x, y+1), (x, y-1)\}$$
These are the four pixels sharing an edge with $p$. Each is at Euclidean distance 1.

**Step 2 — Diagonal Neighbors (corner-touching pixels).**
$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1), (x+1, y-1), (x-1, y+1), (x-1, y-1)\}$$
Each is at Euclidean distance $\sqrt{2}$.

**Step 3 — 8-Neighbors (Union).**
$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$

**Step 4 — Distance Metrics.**
Given two pixels $p = (x, y)$ and $q = (s, t)$:

- **Euclidean Distance ($D_e$ or $L_2$ norm):**
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$$

- **City-Block / Manhattan Distance ($D_4$ or $L_1$ norm):**
$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x - s \vert + \vert y - t \vert$$
Pixels with $D_4(p, q) = 1$ are precisely the 4-neighbors of $p$.

- **Chessboard / Chebyshev Distance ($D_8$ or $L_\infty$ norm):**
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x - s \vert, \vert y - t \vert)$$
Pixels with $D_8(p, q) = 1$ are precisely the 8-neighbors of $p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Connection:** Distance measures are foundational for *region growing, edge detection, and morphological operations* covered in later modules. Memorize the three formulas — they will reappear in every subsequent module.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Sampling Rate and Aliasing Constraint

**Statement:** Prove that the sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to avoid aliasing.

**Step 1 — Define the input signal.**
Let the continuous image have maximum spatial frequency $f_{max}$ (cycles per unit distance).

**Step 2 — Sample at interval $\Delta$.**
Sampling replaces the continuous function with discrete points at $x_n = n \Delta$. The sampling frequency is $f_s = 1/\Delta$.

**Step 3 — Spectral replication.**
Sampling in the spatial domain produces periodic replication of the spectrum in the frequency domain at multiples of $f_s$. To prevent overlap of these replicas:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent spatial sampling density.**
For an image of physical width $W$ mm with $N$ samples across it:
$$\Delta = \frac{W}{N} \Rightarrow N \geq \frac{2 W \cdot f_{max}}{1}$$

**Numerical Example:**
An image has features no smaller than $0.1$ mm. Thus $f_{max} = 1/0.1 = 10$ cycles/mm. To sample a $10$ mm wide region without aliasing:
$$N \geq 2 \cdot 10 \cdot 10 = 200 \text{ samples}$$
A 200-pixel-wide image is the minimum required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a DIP System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the seven canonical blocks of a digital image processing system and the precise direction of data flow between them. Each node represents a functional stage, and each arrow represents the flow of image data (electrical signal, digital matrix, or pixel stream).

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph stage1["Stage 1 Physical World"]
        A1[Scene Illumination Source]
        A2[Reflected Light Energy from Object]
    end

    subgraph stage2["Stage 2 Image Acquisition"]
        B1[Optical Front End Lens]
        B2[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    end

    subgraph stage3["Stage 3 Digitization"]
        C1[Sampler Spatial Discretization]
        C2[Quantizer Intensity Discretization]
        C3[Digital Image Matrix f of x y]
    end

    subgraph stage4["Stage 4 Processing Engine"]
        D1[Digital Computer CPU GPU DSP]
        D2[Image Processing Software Algorithms]
    end

    subgraph stage5["Stage 5 Storage Tier"]
        E1[Short Term RAM Cache]
        E2[Long Term SSD HDD Optical]
    end

    subgraph stage6["Stage 6 Display Tier"]
        F1[Soft Display LCD LED Monitor]
        F2[Hard Display Printer Plotter]
    end

    subgraph stage7["Stage 7 Communication Tier"]
```
