```mermaid
        G1[Network Interface LAN WAN]
        G2[Remote Receiver Server Cloud]
    end

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> E2
    D2 --> F1
    D2 --> F2
    D2 --> G1
    G1 --> G2
    E1 --> D1
    E2 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Reading the Diagram

- **Left-to-Right / Top-to-Bottom Flow:** Energy flows from the physical scene (left) through the processor (middle) to the human observer or remote system (right).
- **Feedback Loop (`E1 → D1`):** Active images in RAM are fed back to the CPU for iterative processing, reflecting the volatile nature of Tier-1 storage.
- **Subgraphs:** Each colored subgraph represents one *logical tier* — students must be able to draw these seven stages from memory in a 14-mark KTU answer.

## 4.3 Simplified Single-Pass Block Diagram (For Quick Recall)

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Image Source] --> opt[Optics Lens]
    opt --> sens[Sensor]
    sens --> dig[Digitizer]
    dig --> cpu[Computer]
    cpu --> store[Storage]
    cpu --> disp[Display]
    cpu --> net[Network]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note:** This Mermaid block represents a *functional architecture flow* rather than a physical circuit. In a viva or written exam, students are expected to draw the physical block diagram with rectangular blocks and labeled arrows indicating *signal type* (analog, sampled, quantized, processed).

## 4.4 Sampling-Quantization Subsystem (Expanded View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    analog[Analog Image Signal] --> samp[Sampler Switched at f sub s]
    samp --> hold[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    hold --> quant[Quantizer k bit ADC]
    quant --> code[Binary Codeword b sub 0 to b sub k minus 1]
    code --> dig[Digital Image f x y]
```

This sub-diagram is critical for Module 1 — examiners often test the **internal structure of the digitizer block** as a 7-mark sub-part.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a Digital Image Processing System. List its fundamental components. [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
A Digital Image Processing System is a coordinated hardware-software framework that captures, digitizes, manipulates, stores, displays, and transmits visual information in numerical (digital) form.

The fundamental components are:
1. **Image Acquisition Subsystem** — Optical lens and image sensor (CCD/CMOS).
2. **Digitizer** — Sampler and quantizer that convert the analog signal to a digital matrix.
3. **Image Processing Hardware** — CPU, GPU, DSP, FPGA/ASIC.
4. **Computer with Software** — Algorithms and operating system.
5. **Mass Storage** — RAM, SSD, HDD, optical media.
6. **Image Display** — Monitor or printer.
7. **Communication Network** — LAN, WAN, or Internet.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + 6–7 components (1 mark for clustering or 2 marks for detailed listing) = 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between Sampling and Quantization. Why are both necessary? [3 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Sampling | Quantization |
|---|---|---|
| Domain of Discretization | Spatial $(x, y)$ | Intensity $f$ |
| Discrete Variable | Pixel position | Gray level value |
| Standard | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $L = 2^k$ levels |
| Continuous → Discrete Mapping | Continuous space → discrete grid | Continuous intensity → integer code |

**Why both are necessary:**
- **Sampling** alone maps the continuous image to a finite grid of pixel positions, but intensity at each pixel is still continuous.
- **Quantization** alone assigns discrete intensity codes, but to a continuum of positions.
- **Both together** are required to produce a *fully discrete* digital image that can be stored in computer memory as a finite matrix of integers.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the fundamental components of a digital image processing system with a neat block diagram. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Model Solution:**

A digital image processing system is a coordinated chain of seven functional blocks that transform real-world visual information into manipulable numerical data and back. The seven blocks are described below.

**1. Image Acquisition (Optical + Sensor):** A physical scene illuminated by an external light source reflects energy, which is focused by an optical lens onto an image sensor. The sensor (CCD or CMOS) converts the focused light into a proportional analog electrical signal at each pixel location. This is the *front-end transducer* stage.

**2. Digitizer:** The analog sensor output is fed into a digitizer that performs two operations:
- **Sampling** — Discretizes the spatial domain. Sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy Nyquist criterion $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$.
- **Quantization** — Discretizes the intensity domain into $L = 2^k$ levels where $k$ is the bit depth.

**3. Computer (Processing Engine):** A general-purpose CPU executes the image processing algorithms. Specialized co-processors (GPU for parallelism, DSP for streaming) may accelerate operations like convolution, FFT, and morphological filtering.

**4. Image Processing Software:** A layered software stack — kernel-level pixel operations, mid-level algorithms (filtering, segmentation, morphology), and high-level application modules (medical diagnosis, biometric identification).

**5. Mass Storage:** A three-tier hierarchy — RAM (volatile, fast), SSD/HDD (online, moderate), and optical/magnetic tape/cloud (archival, slow but high capacity).

**6. Image Display:** The processed digital matrix is converted back into visible form via a soft display (LCD, LED monitor) or a hard display (printer, film recorder).

**7. Communication:** Image data is transmitted over LAN, WAN, or Internet to remote receivers. Compression standards (JPEG, PNG, DICOM) reduce bandwidth requirements.

**Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above — the seven subgraphs are exactly these seven components.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often *omit the network/communication block*, considering it non-essential. In KTU 2024 marking schemes, a missing communication block costs **1 mark** in the 7-mark question. Always draw all seven blocks.

---

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the image acquisition subsystem in detail. Compare CCD and CMOS image sensors. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**Model Solution:**

**Image Acquisition Subsystem Block Diagram:**

*(Refer to the *Stage 2* subgraph from Section 4.1 — the optical front-end feeds into the sensor, which feeds the digitizer.)*

**Working Principle:**
1. The **scene** is illuminated by an external light source (sun, LED, flash, infrared).
2. The **optical front-end** (lens) focuses the reflected light onto the image plane of the sensor.
3. The **image sensor** is a 2-D array of photodiodes. Each photodiode generates a current proportional to the incident light intensity.
4. The accumulated charge at each pixel is read out, amplified, and converted to voltage.
5. This analog voltage signal is forwarded to the digitizer block.

**CCD vs CMOS Comparison:**

| Property | CCD | CMOS |
|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing Process | Specialized process | Standard CMOS process |
| Pixel Architecture | Passive pixel + charge transfer | Active pixel with in-pixel amplifier |
| Readout Method | Sequential bucket-brigade shift | Random access (row/column select) |
| Noise | Lower noise, higher uniformity | Higher noise, fixed-pattern noise |
| Power Consumption | Higher (multiple voltage rails) | Lower (single supply) |
| Speed | Slower readout | Faster readout, parallel |
| Integration | External ADC required | On-chip ADC and control logic |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Application | Scientific, astronomy, medical | Smartphones, webcams, automotive |

**Conclusion:** CCDs remain preferred for applications demanding the highest possible image fidelity and lowest noise (e.g., astrophotography, microscopy), while CMOS dominates the consumer and industrial markets due to its lower power, faster speed, and ability to integrate processing circuits on the same die.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain in detail the digitizer block of a DIP system. State and prove the Nyquist sampling criterion. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Solution:**

**Digitizer Block — Detailed Working:**

The digitizer is the bridge between the analog sensor output and the digital computer. It has two sequential sub-blocks:

1. **Sampler (Spatial Discretization):**
   - Input: Continuous analog signal $g(x)$ from the sensor.
   - Operation: Multiplies $g(x)$ by an impulse train $p(x) = \sum_n \delta(x - n\Delta)$ where $\Delta$ is the sampling interval.
   - Output: Discrete signal $g_s(x) = g(x) \cdot p(x) = \sum_n g(n\Delta) \delta(x - n\Delta)$.
   - Sampling frequency: $f_s = 1/\Delta$.

2. **Sample-and-Hold Circuit:**
   - Holds each sampled value constant for the duration of the quantization step.
   - Prevents voltage drift during ADC conversion.

3. **Quantizer (Intensity Discretization):**
   - Input: Held analog voltage.
   - Operation: Maps the continuous voltage range $[V_{min}, V_{max}]$ to one of $2^k$ discrete codewords.
   - Output: $k$-bit binary integer.
   - Quantization step: $\Delta V = (V_{max} - V_{min}) / (2^k - 1)$.
   - Quantization error: bounded by $\pm \Delta V / 2$.

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem — Proof Sketch:**

Let the input signal have Fourier transform $G(f)$ bandlimited to $\vert f \vert \leq f_{max}$.

Sampling in the spatial domain corresponds to **convolution in the frequency domain** with the Fourier transform of the impulse train $P(f) = (1/\Delta) \sum_n \delta(f - n f_s)$.

The sampled spectrum is:
$$G_s(f) = G(f) * P(f) = \frac{1}{\Delta} \sum_n G(f - n f_s)$$

This is a periodic replication of $G(f)$ centered at $n f_s$.

**Non-overlap condition:** For replicas not to overlap (and thus not to corrupt the spectrum), the gap between adjacent replicas must be non-negative:
$$2 f_{max} \leq f_s$$
Equivalently:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$
This is the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling criterion**. If violated, the replicas overlap and the original signal cannot be uniquely recovered — this corruption is called **aliasing**.

---

#### (b) Define the following terms with respect to a digital image: (i) Neighbors of a pixel, (ii) Connectivity, (iii) Distance measures. State the formulas for Euclidean, City-block, and Chessboard distances. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Neighbors of a Pixel:**

For a pixel $p$ at coordinates $(x, y)$:

- **4-Neighbors:** $N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y), (x-1, y), (x, y+1), (x, y-1)\}$ — four edge-adjacent pixels.
- **Diagonal Neighbors:** $N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1), (x+1, y-1), (x-1, y+1), (x-1, y-1)\}$ — four corner-adjacent pixels.
- **8-Neighbors:** $N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$ — all eight surrounding pixels.

**(ii) Connectivity:**

Two pixels are *connected* if there exists a path of neighboring pixels between them. Connectivity types:
- **4-Connectivity:** Path uses only $N_4$ neighbors.
- **8-Connectivity:** Path uses $N_8$ neighbors.
- **m-Connectivity (Mixed):** Path uses 4-neighbors, with diagonal pixels used only when no 4-connectivity exists *and* their 4-neighbors do not share a common label.

Connectivity is foundational for defining image *regions* and *boundaries*.

**(iii) Distance Measures:**

For two pixels $p = (x, y)$ and $q = (s, t)$:

- **Euclidean Distance ($D_e$):**
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$$
- **City-Block Distance ($D_4$):**
$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x-s \vert + \vert y-t \vert$$
- **Chessboard Distance ($D_8$):**
$$D_8(p, q) = \max\bigl(\vert x-s \vert, \vert y-t \vert\bigr)$$

These three distances satisfy the metric axioms: non-negativity, identity of indiscernibles, symmetry, and triangle inequality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B-b):** Students frequently *omit the metric axioms verification* (1 mark) or *confuse $D_4$ and $D_8$ neighbor sets*. KTU examiners explicitly check whether the student has linked each distance to the corresponding neighbor set. State: "$D_4 = 1$ corresponds to $N_4$ neighbors; $D_8 = 1$ corresponds to $N_8$ neighbors."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Elements of DIP System**

- [ ] A digital image is a discrete function $f(x, y)$ over a finite $M \times N$ grid with $L = 2^k$ intensity levels.
- [ ] A DIP system has **seven canonical blocks**: Acquisition, Digitizer, Computer, Software, Storage, Display, Communication.
- [ ] **Sampling** discretizes *space*; **Quantization** discretizes *intensity*. Both are required for full digitization.
- [ ] Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (no aliasing). Aliasing causes Moiré patterns.
- [ ] Storage formula: $B = M \cdot N \cdot k$ bits (grayscale); $B = M \cdot N \cdot 3k$ bits (24-bit RGB).
- [ ] **CCD** = high fidelity, low noise, slow, high power. **CMOS** = fast, low power, on-chip integration, higher noise.
- [ ] **4-Neighbors** $N_4(p)$: edge-adjacent pixels. **8-Neighbors** $N_8(p)$: edge + corner adjacent. **$N_D$** = corner-only.
- [ ] **Distances:** Euclidean $\sqrt{(x-s)^2 + (y-t)^2}$, City-block $\vert x-s \vert + \vert y-t \vert$, Chessboard $\max(\vert x-s \vert, \vert y-t \vert)$.
- [ ] **Connectivity types:** 4-, 8-, and m-connectivity. m-connectivity avoids multiple-path ambiguity.
- [ ] **Storage hierarchy:** RAM (volatile fast) → SSD/HDD (online) → Optical/Tape/Cloud (archival).
- [ ] **Compression standards** (JPEG, PNG, DICOM) sit inside the communication block.
- [ ] A standard 8-bit grayscale image occupies 1 byte/pixel; a 24-bit color image occupies 3 bytes/pixel.
- [ ] For an $N \times N$ image, the total pixel count is $N^2$; storage in bytes is $N^2 \cdot k / 8$.
- [ ] Always include the **Communication block** in a 7-block DIP diagram — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if missing.
- [ ] The digitizer is the *only* block that performs both sampling and quantization — never confuse its role with the sensor or the computer.
- [ ] Module 1 vocabulary (neighbors, distance, connectivity) reappears verbatim in Modules 2, 3, 4, and 5 — internalize it once and reuse forever.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Elements of DIP System.** Proceed to next sub-topic: *Image Sampling and Quantization* for deeper treatment of $L$, $k$, and isopreference curves.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Relationship Between Pixels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Relationship Between Pixels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A digital image is a 2D discrete function $f(x, y)$ where $x$ and $y$ are spatial (integer) coordinates and $f$ is the intensity (gray level) at that coordinate. Each point $(x, y)$ in this discrete grid is called a **pixel** (picture element), and pixels are the smallest addressable unit of a digital image.

The **basic relationships between pixels** describe how a given pixel is geometrically and logically connected to the pixels surrounding it. These relationships form the *mathematical foundation* for nearly every operation in image processing, including **edge detection, segmentation, morphological operations, and connected-component labeling**.

The three fundamental relationship categories are:

1. **Neighborhoods of a pixel** — which pixels are physically next to it.
2. **Adjacency, Connectivity, and Paths** — which pixels can be reached from a given pixel by traversing "neighboring" pixels of similar intensity.
3. **Distance Measures** — quantitative metrics (Euclidean, City-Block, Chessboard) that quantify how "far" two pixels are from each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 1):**
> The relationships covered here are **prerequisites** for Modules 2, 3, and 4 — particularly for *image segmentation* (region growing), *boundary detection*, and *morphological processing*. Examiner questions on this topic are very frequent and form the base for higher-order derivations.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Pixel City"**
> Imagine a city laid out as a perfect grid of square houses, exactly like a chessboard. Each house (pixel) has a unique address $(x, y)$.
> - The **4 neighbors** of a house are the houses directly to its **North, South, East, and West** (sharing a common wall). 
> - The **diagonal neighbors** are the houses at its **NE, NW, SE, SW** corners (sharing only a corner-point).
> - The **8 neighbors** = 4 neighbors + 4 diagonal neighbors (every house that physically touches the central house).
> - **Adjacency** is like saying "two houses are friends only if they share a wall or corner AND belong to the same club (gray level)."
> - **Distance** is the cost of walking from one house to another. The **city-block distance** measures how many blocks you walk (Manhattan style). The **chessboard distance** measures the minimum number of king-moves on a chessboard. The **Euclidean distance** is the "as-the-crow-flies" straight-line distance.

This simple grid analogy makes every concept below intuitive. A student should always picture a chessboard-like lattice when reading this section.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Symbols

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $p = (x, y)$ | A pixel at integer coordinate $(x, y)$ |
| $V$ | The set of gray-level values used to define adjacency |
| $N_4(p)$ | Set of 4-neighbors of $p$ |
| $N_D(p)$ | Set of 4 diagonal-neighbors of $p$ |
| $N_8(p)$ | Set of 8-neighbors of $p$ |
| $D_e, D_4, D_8$ | Euclidean, City-Block, and Chessboard distances |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Neighborhoods of a Pixel

For a pixel $p$ at coordinate $(x, y)$:

### 2.1.1 4-Neighborhood ($N_4$)
The set of **4-neighbors** consists of the four pixels sharing an edge with $p$:

$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

> **Why it matters:** $N_4$ defines the *minimum connectivity* between pixels. Every "4-connected path" between two pixels uses only these neighbors.

### 2.1.2 Diagonal Neighborhood ($N_D$)
The set of **diagonal neighbors** consists of the four pixels sharing only a corner with $p$:

$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

> **Why it matters:** $N_D$ alone is rarely used; it is *added* to $N_4$ to form $N_8$.

### 2.1.3 8-Neighborhood ($N_8$)
Combining $N_4$ and $N_D$:

$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$

giving **8 neighbors** in total (every pixel physically adjacent to $p$).

> **Real-world utility:** $N_8$ is the basis for 8-connected component analysis used in object detection, OCR character segmentation, and morphological dilation/erosion.

---

## 2.2 Adjacency

Let $V$ be the set of gray-level values used to define adjacency (e.g., for binary images, $V = \{1\}$ for foreground).

Two pixels $p$ and $q$, each taking values from $V$, are said to be:

### 2.2.1 4-Adjacent
$p$ and $q$ are **4-adjacent** if $q \in N_4(p)$.

### 2.2.2 8-Adjacent
$p$ and $q$ are **8-adjacent** if $q \in N_8(p)$.

### 2.2.3 m-Adjacent (Mixed Adjacency)
$p$ and $q$ are **m-adjacent** if **any one** of the following conditions is satisfied:
1. $q \in N_4(p)$, **OR**
2. $q \in N_D(p)$ **AND** $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q)$ has **no pixels whose gray-level values are in $V$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' of m-Adjacency:**
> 8-adjacency can create **ambiguous paths** (e.g., two diagonal pixels connect through a common neighbor, but the common neighbor is not part of $V$). m-adjacency *eliminates this ambiguity* by forbidding diagonal connections when a 4-connected alternative is possible. It is widely used in **region-growing segmentation**.

---

## 2.3 Connectivity, Paths, and Regions

- A **path** from pixel $p$ to pixel $q$ is a sequence of distinct pixels $p = p_0, p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n = q$ such that $p_i$ is adjacent to $p_{i+1}$ for all $i$. The **length** of the path is $n$.
- A path is said to be **4-path, 8-path, or m-path** depending on the adjacency definition used.
- Two pixels $p$ and $q$ are said to be **connected** (4-connected, 8-connected, or m-connected) if there exists at least one path between them of the corresponding type.
- A **region** $R$ is a connected set of pixels (under a chosen adjacency).
- The **boundary** of a region $R$ is the set of pixels in $R$ that have at least one neighbor *outside* $R$.

> **Engineering utility:** Used in image segmentation, region labeling, contour tracing, and watershed algorithms.

---

## 2.4 Distance Measures

For pixels $p = (x_1, y_1)$ and $q = (x_2, y_2)$:

- **Euclidean Distance ($D_e$):** Straight-line "as the crow flies" distance.
- **City-Block Distance ($D_4$):** Sum of absolute coordinate differences — Manhattan distance.
- **Chessboard Distance ($D_8$):** Maximum of absolute coordinate differences — Chebyshev distance.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **board-exam essential**. Memorize these three formulas, the three adjacency types, and the two neighborhood definitions. They appear in nearly every ESE Module 1 paper.

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Geometric Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $N_4(p)$ | $\{(x\pm 1, y),\ (x, y\pm 1)\}$ | 4 edge-adjacent pixels |
| $N_D(p)$ | $\{(x\pm 1, y\pm 1)\}$ | 4 corner-adjacent pixels |
| $N_8(p)$ | $N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$ | All 8 adjacent pixels |
| Euclidean $D_e(p, q)$ | $\sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$ | Straight-line (Pythagoras) |
| City-Block $D_4(p, q)$ | $\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | Manhattan blocks walked |
| Chessboard $D_8(p, q)$ | $\max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert,\ \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$ | Min. king-moves on chessboard |
| m-adjacency | Diagonal link allowed only if $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q) \cap V = \emptyset$ | Removes path ambiguity |
| Boundary of $R$ | $R$ pixels with at least one neighbor outside $R$ | Outer edge pixels |

> **Real-world utility:** $D_4$ and $D_8$ are used in fast image-matching and template-search because they are **integer-valued** and require no square root — making them **computationally cheaper** than $D_e$. $D_e$ is preferred when geometric accuracy is critical (e.g., camera calibration, medical imaging).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Finding All Neighbors

**Problem:** Given a pixel $p = (3, 4)$, find $N_4(p)$, $N_D(p)$, and $N_8(p)$.

**Solution:**

Using the definition $N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$:

$$N_4(3, 4) = \{(3+1, 4),\ (3-1, 4),\ (3, 4+1),\ (3, 4-1)\}$$
$$N_4(3, 4) = \{(4, 4),\ (2, 4),\ (3, 5),\ (3, 3)\}$$

Using the definition $N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$:

$$N_D(3, 4) = \{(4, 5),\ (4, 3),\ (2, 5),\ (2, 3)\}$$

Now combine them:

$$N_8(3, 4) = \{(2, 3),\ (2, 4),\ (2, 5),\ (3, 3),\ (3, 5),\ (4, 3),\ (4, 4),\ (4, 5)\}$$

> **Verification logic:** Count the result — exactly 8 distinct pixels, confirming the formula's correctness.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Distance Calculations

**Problem:** Compute $D_e$, $D_4$, and $D_8$ between $p = (1, 2)$ and $q = (4, 6)$.

### Step 1: Compute the coordinate differences

$$\Delta x = x_1 - x_2 = 1 - 4 = -3$$
$$\Delta y = y_1 - y_2 = 2 - 6 = -4$$

### Step 2: Compute Euclidean distance

$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(1 - 4)^2 + (2 - 6)^2}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(-3)^2 + (-4)^2}$$
$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$$

### Step 3: Compute City-Block distance

$$D_4(p, q) = \vert 1 - 4 \vert + \vert 2 - 6 \vert$$
$$D_4(p, q) = 3 + 4 = 7$$

### Step 4: Compute Chessboard distance

$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert 1 - 4 \vert,\ \vert 2 - 6 \vert)$$
$$D_8(p, q) = \max(3, 4) = 4$$

> **Result summary:** $D_e = 5.00$, $D_4 = 7$, $D_8 = 4$. Note that **$D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$** always holds — a useful sanity check during exams.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Identifying m-Adjacency

**Problem:** Consider a 3×3 image patch (rows and columns 1–3). Pixels at $(1,1), (1,3), (3,1), (3,3)$ have value **1** (foreground). The center pixel $(2,2)$ has value **0**, and the four edge-centers $(1,2), (2,1), (2,3), (3,2)$ have value **0**. Let $V = \{1\}$. Is $(1,1)$ m-adjacent to $(1,3)$?

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Check if $(1,3) \in N_4(1,1)$.

$$N_4(1, 1) = \{(0, 1),\ (2, 1),\ (1, 0),\ (1, 2)\}$$
$$(1, 3) \notin N_4(1, 1)$$

So the first m-adjacency condition fails. We must check the diagonal condition.

**Step 2:** Check if $(1,3) \in N_D(1,1)$.

$$N_D(1, 1) = \{(0, 0),\ (0, 2),\ (2, 0),\ (2, 2)\}$$
$$(1, 3) \notin N_D(1, 1)$$

So $(1,1)$ and $(1,3)$ are **not m-adjacent** either. They would, however, be **8-adjacent** (since one can traverse the diagonal pixels in 8-adjacency). This is exactly the kind of question KTU examiners use to test the difference between 8- and m-adjacency.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Connectivity & Path

**Problem:** A 4×4 binary image has the value **1** at coordinates $(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (3,3), (4,4)$. Is there a **4-path** from $(1,1)$ to $(3,3)$?

**Solution:**

From $(1,1)$, the only 4-connected pixels with value 1 are $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$. From either of these, the next 4-connected pixel with value 1 must lie in $N_4$ of the current pixel.

- $(1,2)$ has 4-neighbors $\{(0,2), (2,2), (1,1), (1,3)\}$ — none of the new ones have value 1.
- $(2,1)$ has 4-neighbors $\{(1,1), (3,1), (2,0), (2,2)\}$ — only $(3,1)$ has value 1.
- $(3,1)$ has 4-neighbors $\{(2,1), (4,1), (3,0), (3,2)\}$ — none of the new ones have value 1.

We cannot reach $(3,3)$ via 4-path. Under **8-path**, however, we can go $(1,1) \to (2,1) \to (3,3)$ (diagonal jump), since $(3,3) \in N_8(2,1)$.

> **Insight:** Connectivity depends entirely on the adjacency definition used. This is the conceptual "trap" KTU examiners test.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pixel Neighborhood Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Pixel p at x,y"] --> B["N4 Neighbors"]
    A --> C["ND Diagonal Neighbors"]
    A --> D["N8 Combined"]

    B --> B1["North x, y-1"]
    B --> B2["South x, y+1"]
    B --> B3["East x+1, y"]
    B --> B4["West x-1, y"]

    C --> C1["NE x+1, y-1"]
    C --> C2["NW x-1, y-1"]
    C --> C3["SE x+1, y+1"]
    C --> C4["SW x-1, y+1"]

    D --> E["All 8 pixels in N4 and ND"]
    D --> F["Used in 8 adjacency and 8 path"]
    D --> G["Foundation for connectivity checks"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A central pixel $p$ decomposes into two disjoint neighbor sets — the 4 edge-adjacent ones ($N_4$) and the 4 corner-adjacent ones ($N_D$). Their union forms $N_8$.

---

## 4.2 Adjacency Types Comparison (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Pixel p"] -- N4 only --> A1["4 Adjacency"]
    P1 -- N8 includes diagonals --> A2["8 Adjacency"]
    P1 -- N4 plus restricted diagonals --> A3["m Adjacency"]

    A2 --> R1["Can have multiple paths"]
    A3 --> R2["Unique path guaranteed"]
    A1 --> R3["No diagonal jumps allowed"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** $N_4$ adjacency is the most restrictive (no diagonal jumps). 8-adjacency is the most permissive. m-adjacency strikes a balance — it is more permissive than 4-adjacency but eliminates the multiple-path ambiguity of 8-adjacency.

---

## 4.3 Distance Measure Visual (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Input p1, p2 coordinates"] --> S2["Compute dx and dy"]
    S2 --> S3["Branch A sqrt dx2 + dy2"]
    S2 --> S4["Branch B sum of abs dx and dy"]
    S2 --> S5["Branch C max of abs dx, dy"]
    S3 --> O1["Euclidean Distance"]
    S4 --> O2["City Block D4"]
    S5 --> O3["Chessboard D8"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** All three distance measures begin from the same two-pixel input, branch on the formula used, and yield three different scalar distances. Note that $D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$ is **always** true.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define 4-adjacency, 8-adjacency, and m-adjacency between two pixels. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Let $p$ be a pixel with coordinate $(x, y)$ and $V$ be the set of gray-level values defining adjacency. Two pixels $p$ and $q$ (both with values in $V$) are said to be adjacent if:

- **4-adjacency:** $q \in N_4(p)$ — i.e., $q$ is one of the four edge-adjacent neighbors of $p$.
- **8-adjacency:** $q \in N_8(p)$ — i.e., $q$ is one of the eight neighbors (edges + diagonals) of $p$.
- **m-adjacency:** $q \in N_4(p)$, **OR** $q \in N_D(p)$ **AND** $N_4(p) \cap N_4(q) \cap V = \emptyset$ (no foreground pixel exists at the common 4-neighbor).

m-adjacency is used to **eliminate the ambiguity** of multiple paths that can arise in 8-adjacency.

---

### Question 2. List the three distance measures used in digital image processing and write their formulas. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

For two pixels $p = (x_1, y_1)$ and $q = (x_2, y_2)$:

- **Euclidean distance** $D_e$: $\sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$
- **City-Block distance** $D_4$: $\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$
- **Chessboard distance** $D_8$: $\max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert,\ \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A

**(a) Explain the different types of pixel neighborhoods with neat diagrams. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a pixel $p$ at location $(x, y)$, three neighborhood types are defined.

**1. 4-Neighborhood ($N_4$):** Contains the four pixels that share an edge with $p$.

$$N_4(p) = \{(x+1, y),\ (x-1, y),\ (x, y+1),\ (x, y-1)\}$$

These are: East, West, North, and South pixels.

**2. Diagonal Neighborhood ($N_D$):** Contains the four pixels that share a corner with $p$.

$$N_D(p) = \{(x+1, y+1),\ (x+1, y-1),\ (x-1, y+1),\ (x-1, y-1)\}$$

**3. 8-Neighborhood ($N_8$):** Union of $N_4$ and $N_D$, giving 8 total neighbors.

$$N_8(p) = N_4(p) \cup N_D(p)$$

**Diagram (text representation):**

| | $x-1$ | $x$ | $x+1$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $y-1$ | NW ($N_D$) | N ($N_4$) | NE ($N_D$) |
| $y$ | W ($N_4$) | **p** | E ($N_4$) |
| $y+1$ | SW ($N_D$) | S ($N_4$) | SE ($N_D$) |

This grid is centered on $p$ at $(x, y)$. The four edge cells form $N_4$, the four corner cells form $N_D$, and all eight form $N_8$.

---

**(b) Define connectivity, path, and region. With a binary image, explain 4-connectivity, 8-connectivity, and m-connectivity with an example. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**
- A **path** from $p$ to $q$ is a sequence of distinct pixels $p_0, p_1, \ldots, p_n$ such that each $p_i$ is adjacent to $p_{i+1}$. The length is $n$.
- Two pixels are **connected** if at least one path exists between them.
- A **region** is a set of pixels in which every pair is connected.

**Example — 3×3 image patch:**

| | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Row 1** | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| **Row 2** | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **Row 3** | 1 | 0 | 1 |

Let $V = \{1\}$. We want to know if pixel $(1,1)$ is connected to $(1,3)$.

- **4-connectivity:** $(1,1) \to$ must move only North/South/East/West through value-1 pixels. The only path of value-1 neighbors is blocked because $(1,2) = 0$. **Not 4-connected.**
- **8-connectivity:** Diagonal moves allowed. $(1,1) \to (2,2) \to (1,3)$ is valid because $(2,2) \in N_8(1,1)$ and $(1,3) \in N_8(2,2)$. **8-connected.**
- **m-connectivity:** Check the diagonal condition. The common 4-neighbor of $(1,1)$ and $(2,2)$ is $\{(1,2), (2,1)\}$, both of which are 0 (not in $V$). So $(2,2)$ can serve as a "bridge". Similarly, common 4-neighbor of $(2,2)$ and $(1,3)$ is $\{(1,2), (2,3)\}$ — both 0. So the path is **m-valid**.

> **Conclusion:** In this case, 8-connectivity and m-connectivity give the same result, but the m-path is **unique**, while the 8-path is **ambiguous** (two distinct 8-paths exist).

---

### Question B

**(a) Explain Euclidean, City-Block, and Chessboard distance measures with formulas. Compute all three for $p = (2, 3)$ and $q = (7, 9)$. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Euclidean Distance** — Pythagoras theorem in 2D:

$$D_e(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$$

It is the straight-line, "as-the-crow-flies" distance.

**2. City-Block Distance ($D_4$)** — Manhattan distance:

$$D_4(p, q) = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$$

It represents the number of horizontal/vertical moves required to go from $p$ to $q$.

**3. Chessboard Distance ($D_8$)** — Chebyshev distance:

$$D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert,\ \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$$

It is the minimum number of king-moves needed on a chessboard.

**Numerical Computation** for $p = (2, 3)$ and $q = (7, 9)$:

Step 1 — Differences:

$$\Delta x = 2 - 7 = -5, \quad \Delta y = 3 - 9 = -6$$

Step 2 — Euclidean:

$$D_e = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + (-6)^2} = \sqrt{25 + 36} = \sqrt{61} \approx 7.81$$

Step 3 — City-Block:

$$D_4 = \vert -5 \vert + \vert -6 \vert = 5 + 6 = 11$$

Step 4 — Chessboard:

$$D_8 = \max(5, 6) = 6$$

**Verification:** $D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$ — i.e., $6 \le 7.81 \le 11$. ✓

---

**(b) Differentiate between 8-adjacency and m-adjacency. Construct a 3×3 example where the two definitions yield different results. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference in Definition:**

| Property | 8-Adjacency | m-Adjacency |
|---|---|---|
| Diagonal moves | Always allowed | Allowed only if 4-neighbor of both endpoints (in $V$) is empty |
| Multiple paths | Can yield multiple paths between same pair | Guarantees a unique path |
| Use case | Object detection, morphological ops | Region growing, segmentation |

**Constructed Example:**

Consider the 3×3 image:

| | Col 1 | Col 2 | Col 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Row 1** | 1 | 1 | 0 |
| **Row 2** | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| **Row 3** | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Let $V = \{1\}$. We examine whether $(1,1)$ is connected to $(2,2)$.

- **8-adjacency:** $(1,1) \to (2,2)$ is a single diagonal move. Since $(2,2) \in N_8(1,1)$ and both have value 1, they are **8-adjacent**.
- **m-adjacency:** $N_4(1,1) = \{(0,1), (2,1), (1,0), (1,2)\}$ and $N_4(2,2) = \{(1,2), (3,2), (2,1), (2,3)\}$. Their intersection is $\{(1,2), (2,1)\}$. Both $(1,2)$ and $(2,1)$ have value **1**, which is in $V$. So the diagonal link is **forbidden** under m-adjacency. **Not m-adjacent.**

**Conclusion:** This is a classic case where 8- and m-adjacency differ — a direct diagonal connection is allowed under 8-adjacency but rejected under m-adjacency because a 4-connected alternative path exists.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Common Mark-Deduction Zones):**
> 1. **Confusing $N_4$ with $N_8$:** Writing $N_4$ formula with 8 entries costs full marks on the "neighborhood" question.
> 2. **Forgetting the $V$ constraint in m-adjacency:** Many students define m-adjacency as "diagonal link" only, missing the crucial *intersection with $V$* check.
> 3. **Mixing up $D_4$ and $D_8$:** $D_4$ is the **sum**, $D_8$ is the **max**. Interchanging them is the most common error.
> 4. **Forgetting the inequality $D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$:** Examiners often expect this sanity check in distance numericals — skipping it costs partial credit.
> 5. **Not drawing the pixel grid:** For "neighbors" and "connectivity" questions, **a labeled 3×3 grid is mandatory**. Skipping the diagram typically costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- A pixel $p = (x, y)$ has **4 edge-neighbors** $N_4$, **4 diagonal-neighbors** $N_D$, and **8 total neighbors** $N_8$.
- **Adjacency** is defined between pixels of similar gray-level (within a set $V$).
- **4-adjacency:** only $N_4$ traversal.
- **8-adjacency:** $N_8$ traversal (allows diagonals).
- **m-adjacency:** diagonal link **only** if no common 4-neighbor is in $V$ (eliminates path ambiguity).
- A **path** is a sequence of adjacent pixels; **connectivity** exists when at least one such path exists.
- A **region** is a set of mutually connected pixels; its **boundary** has at least one neighbor outside.
- **$D_e$** uses the square root, **$D_4$** uses sum, **$D_8$** uses max.
- **Inequality to remember:** $D_8 \le D_e \le D_4$ (always true for any two pixels).
- $D_4$ and $D_8$ are **integer-valued** and **computationally cheap** (no square root) — preferred in real-time applications.
- $D_e$ is the only metric that satisfies true geometric properties (rotation invariance).
- m-adjacency is the **preferred adjacency for region-growing segmentation** because it prevents multiple-path ambiguity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Distance Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Distance Measures ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In Digital Image Processing (DIP), a **distance measure** (or **distance metric**) is a scalar, non-negative function $D(p, q)$ that quantifies the spatial separation between two pixels $p$ and $q$ in a discrete 2-D image grid. For a function $D$ to be a valid distance metric, it must satisfy four fundamental axioms for all pixels $p$, $q$, and $r$: 1. **Non-negativity:** $D(p, q) \geq 0$ 2. **Identity of Indiscernibles:** $D(p, q) = 0 \iff p = q$ 3. **Symmetry:** $D(p, q) = D(q, p)$ 4. **Triangle Inequality:** $D(p, r) \leq D(p, q) + D(q, r)$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "A distance measure $D$ between pixels $p$ and $q$ is a real-valued function that satisfies the metric properties of non-negativity, symmetry, and the triangle inequality." For two pixels with coordinates $p = (x_1, y_1)$ and $q = (x_2, y_2)$, the general **Minkowski distance** of order $r$ is given by: $$ D_r(p, q) = \left( \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert^r + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert^r \right)^{1/r} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Different integer values of $r$ yield the **three foundational KTU distance measures** that you MUST memorize: $r = 1$ (City Block), $r = 2$ (Euclidean), and $r = \infty$ (Chessboard). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing at the corner of a city block in **Manhattan, New York**, and you want to reach your friend standing at another corner. The grid of streets is laid out in perfect rectangles, with skyscrapers blocking any straight-line path through buildings. - If you could take a **helicopter** and fly directly over the buildings, the shortest path is the **Euclidean distance** (the "as the crow flies" straight line). - If you must walk along the **sidewalks**, weaving through avenues and streets, you are forced to travel in horizontal and vertical segments only. This is the **City Block (Manhattan) distance**. - If your friend is a **King on a chessboard**, he can move one step in any of the 8 surrounding directions, so the distance is determined by the **maximum** of the coordinate differences — this is the **Chessboard (Chebyshev) distance**. > [!TIP] > **Real-World Engineering Use-Case:** Distance measures are the geometric backbone of **medical image segmentation** (isolating tumors in MRI scans), **autonomous vehicle perception** (clustering LiDAR points), **forensic fingerprint matching**, and **morphological thinning/skeletonization** in OCR systems. Without a well-defined pixel distance, you cannot define what "neighboring" or "nearby" even means in an image. ### 1.3 Why Not Just Use Euclidean Always? While Euclidean distance is mathematically the "true" geometric distance, in a digital image the pixels are arranged on a **discrete integer grid**. The Euclidean distance produces irrational values (square roots) for diagonal moves, which is computationally expensive and inconsistent with the natural 4-connectivity or 8-connectivity neighborhoods of digital images. This is why we use **discrete approximations** of Euclidean distance. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Distance Measures The five principal distance measures covered in KTU Module 1 are: 1. **Euclidean Distance ($D_E$)** — the true geometric distance. 2. **City Block / Manhattan Distance ($D_4$)** — 4-connected neighborhood. 3. **Chessboard / Chebyshev Distance ($D_8$)** — 8-connected neighborhood. 4. **Quasi-Euclidean Distance** — hybrid horizontal/vertical plus diagonal. 5. **Chamfer Distance** — weighted approximation using integer weights. ### 2.2 The Three Core Metrics — Deep Dive #### A) Euclidean Distance ($D_E$) The most accurate geometric distance. It treats every pair of pixels as being connected by a straight line in continuous space. $$ D_E(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2} $$ - **Pros:** Mathematically correct; rotation-invariant. - **Cons:** Computationally expensive (requires square root); produces non-integer values; ignores the discrete 4-/8-connectivity structure of the pixel grid. - **Pixels at $D_E \leq 2$** form a **circle** of radius 2 around the center. #### B) City Block Distance ($D_4$) — L1 Norm Also called the **Manhattan distance** or **taxicab distance**. Pixels are reached by traversing only horizontal and vertical steps along the 4-connected neighborhood. $$ D_4(p, q) = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert $$ - **Pros:** Integer-valued; computationally cheap; matches 4-connectivity. - **Cons:** Overestimates true geometric distance; not rotation-invariant. - **Pixels at $D_4 \leq 2$** form a **diamond** (rhombus) centered on the origin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Tip:** $D_4$ is always the *sum* of absolute coordinate differences. Memorize this. The number "4" comes from the fact that the 4-connected neighbors of a pixel are at $D_4 = 1$. #### C) Chessboard Distance ($D_8$) — L∞ Norm Also called the **Chebyshev distance**. Pixels are reached by the maximum step in either the $x$ or $y$ direction, matching the 8-connected neighborhood (where diagonals count as 1 step, like a King on a chessboard). $$ D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert) $$ - **Pros:** Integer-valued; matches 8-connectivity; very fast. - **Cons:** Underestimates true geometric distance for far-apart pixels. - **Pixels at $D_8 \leq 2$** form a **square** (axis-aligned) centered on the origin. #### D) Quasi-Euclidean Distance A hybrid that uses true Euclidean distance along horizontal/vertical/horizontal-then-vertical paths. For example, to travel from $p$ to $q$, the path takes horizontal and vertical steps with diagonals computed geometrically. $$ D_{QE}(p, q) = \begin{cases} \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert & \text{if } \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert \geq \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert \\ (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ #### E) Chamfer Distance (Weighted Approximation) A fast, integer-only approximation to Euclidean distance. Weights are assigned to horizontal/vertical (short) and diagonal (long) moves. Common weight pairs: - **3-4 Chamfer:** Weights (3, 4) for (horizontal/vertical, diagonal). Maximum error ≈ 8%. - **5-7-11 Chamfer:** Weights (5, 7, 11) for (horizontal/vertical, diagonal, knight-move). Maximum error ≈ 2%. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All absolute value bars in the table below are rendered as `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. In your exam paper, write them as standard $\vert \cdot \vert$ bars. | **Distance Measure** | **Formula** | **Neighborhood** | **Shape at radius $k$** | **Value Range** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Euclidean $D_E$ | $\sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2}$ | Continuous | Circle (radius $k$) | Real numbers $[0, \infty)$ | | City Block $D_4$ | $\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | 4-connected | Diamond (rhombus) | Integers $[0, \infty)$ | | Chessboard $D_8$ | $\max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$ | 8-connected | Square (axis-aligned) | Integers $[0, \infty)$ | | Quasi-Euclidean $D_{QE}$ | Path-based geometric hybrid | Diagonal-aware | Octagonal approximation | Real numbers $[0, \infty)$ | | Chamfer 3-4 | $3 \cdot a + 4 \cdot b$ (a = H/V steps, b = diag steps) | 8-connected | Octagonal approximation | Integers $[0, \infty)$ | | Chamfer 5-7-11 | $5 \cdot a + 7 \cdot b + 11 \cdot c$ | 8-connected + knight | Smooth circular approximation | Integers $[0, \infty)$ | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility of Each Measure | **Application Domain** | **Preferred Distance** | **Reason** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Morphological skeletonization | $D_4$ or $D_8$ | Binary connectivity required | | Blob/region growing | $D_4$ or $D_8$ | Avoids floating-point computation | | Edge-based segmentation | $D_E$ | True geometric boundary detection | | Template matching (rotated objects) | $D_E$ | Rotation-invariance required | | Fast distance transform | Chamfer 3-4 or 5-7-11 | Integer-only, very fast, BFS-based | | Real-time robotics / SLAM | Chamfer 3-4 | Computational efficiency critical | --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example — All Distances for a Center Pixel **Problem Statement:** Consider a center pixel $p = (5, 5)$ in an 8-bit grayscale image. Compute $D_E$, $D_4$, $D_8$, and $D_{QE}$ from $p$ to the following 6 target pixels: - $q_1 = (5, 6)$ — one pixel below - $q_2 = (6, 7)$ — one diagonal down-right - $q_3 = (3, 5)$ — two pixels to the left - $q_4 = (3, 8)$ — far off diagonal - $q_5 = (7, 7)$ — diagonal offset - $q_6 = (5, 5)$ — same as $p$ ### 3.2 Solution Table with Step-by-Step Evaluation For each pixel, we apply the formulas. Let $\Delta x = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$. > **Setup:** For all targets, $\Delta x = \vert 5 - x_2 \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert 5 - y_2 \vert$. | **Target Pixel** | $\Delta x$ | $\Delta y$ | $D_4 = \Delta x + \Delta y$ | $D_8 = \max(\Delta x, \Delta y)$ | $D_E = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | $D_{QE}$ | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $q_1 = (5, 6)$ | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1.000 | 1.000 | | $q_2 = (6, 7)$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | $\sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$ | 2.414 | | $q_3 = (3, 5)$ | 2 | 0 | 2 | 2 | 2.000 | 2.000 | | $q_4 = (3, 8)$ | 2 | 3 | 5 | 3 | $\sqrt{13} \approx 3.606$ | 4.414 | | $q_5 = (7, 7)$ | 2 | 2 | 4 | 2 | $\sqrt{8} \approx 2.828$ | 3.414 | | $q_6 = (5, 5)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0.000 | 0.000 | ### 3.3 Detailed Step-by-Step Calculations (Sample Subset) **Case 1: Distance to $q_2 = (6, 7)$** Step 1: Compute coordinate differences. $$ \Delta x = \vert 5 - 6 \vert = 1, \qquad \Delta y = \vert 5 - 7 \vert = 2 $$ Step 2: Compute $D_4$. $$ D_4 = \vert 5 - 6 \vert + \vert 5 - 7 \vert = 1 + 2 = 3 $$ Step 3: Compute $D_8$. $$ D_8 = \max(1, 2) = 2 $$ Step 4: Compute $D_E$. $$ D_E = \sqrt{1^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{1 + 4} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236 $$ Step 5: Compute $D_{QE}$. Since $\Delta y > \Delta x$ (i.e., $2 > 1$), the horizontal segment of length 1 is computed as true horizontal (cost 1), and the remaining vertical distance of $\vert \Delta y - \Delta x \vert = 1$ is traversed diagonally. $$ D_{QE} = 1 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 2 = 1 + 1.414 = 2.414 $$ **Case 2: Distance to $q_4 = (3, 8)$** Step 1: Compute coordinate differences. $$ \Delta x = \vert 5 - 3 \vert = 2, \qquad \Delta y = \vert 5 - 8 \vert = 3 $$ Step 2: Compute $D_4$. $$ D_4 = 2 + 3 = 5 $$ Step 3: Compute $D_8$. $$ D_8 = \max(2, 3) = 3 $$ Step 4: Compute $D_E$. $$ D_E = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 $$ Step 5: Compute $D_{QE}$. $$ D_{QE} = 2 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 3 = 2 + 1.2426 = 3.243 $$ ### 3.4 Chamfer 3-4 Distance — BFS Style Derivation **Setup:** A "seed" pixel $p = (0, 0)$ has distance 0. We propagate using the recurrence: $$ D(p, q) = \min \begin{cases} D(p, q-1) + 3 & \text{(left neighbor)} \\ D(p, q+1) + 3 & \text{(right neighbor)} \\ D(p-1, q) + 3 & \text{(top neighbor)} \\ D(p+1, q) + 3 & \text{(bottom neighbor)} \\ D(p-1, q-1) + 4 & \text{(top-left diag)} \\ D(p-1, q+1) + 4 & \text{(top-right diag)} \\ D(p+1, q-1) + 4 & \text{(bottom-left diag)} \\ D(p+1, q+1) + 4 & \text{(bottom-right diag)} \end{cases} $$ For the 5x5 grid centered at $(0,0)$ with Chamfer 3-4 weights: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Layer 1:} \quad & D((0,0)) = 0, \; D(\text{4-neighbors}) = 3, \; D(\text{diagonal}) = 4 \\ \text{Layer 2:} \quad & D(\text{2 H/V steps}) = 6, \; D(\text{1 H/V + 1 diag}) = 7, \; D(\text{2 diags}) = 8 \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.5 Comparative Analysis Matrix | **Property** | $D_4$ | $D_8$ | $D_E$ | $D_{QE}$ | Chamfer 3-4 | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Integer-only? | Yes | Yes | No | No | Yes | | Matches 4-connectivity? | Yes | No | No | No | No | | Matches 8-connectivity? | No | Yes | No | No | Yes | | True circular shape? | No (diamond) | No (square) | Yes (circle) | Approx. | Approx. | | Rotation-invariant? | No | No | Yes | Approx. | Approx. | | Computational cost | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ + sqrt | $O(1)$ + sqrt | $O(1)$ | | Max approximation error vs $D_E$ | +41.4% | -29.3% | 0% | +12% | +8% | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Classification Flow of Distance Measures ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Pixel Distance Measures in DIP] --> B[Minkowski Family Lr] A --> C[Path-Based Family] B --> B1["r = 1
City Block D4"] B --> B2["r = 2
Euclidean DE"] B --> B3["r = infinity
Chessboard D8"] C --> C1[Quasi-Euclidean DQE] C --> C2[Chamfer Distance] C2 --> C2a["3-4 Weights
Fast approx"] C2 --> C2b["5-7-11 Weights
Knight-move approx"] B1 --> P1[4-Connectivity] B3 --> P2[8-Connectivity] B2 --> P3[True Geometric] C1 --> P4[Octagonal Shape] C2a --> P4 C2b --> P4 ``` ### 4.2 Neighborhood Connectivity — D4 vs D8 Visualization (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR Center[Center Pixel p] Center --> N1[Top
D4 = 1, D8 = 1, DE = 1] Center --> N2[Bottom
D4 = 1, D8 = 1, DE = 1] Center --> N3[Left
D4 = 1, D8 = 1, DE = 1] Center --> N4[Right
D4 = 1, D8 = 1, DE = 1] Center --> N5[TopLeft Diag
D4 = 2, D8 = 1, DE = 1.414] Center --> N6[TopRight Diag
D4 = 2, D8 = 1, DE = 1.414] Center --> N7[BottomLeft Diag
D4 = 2, D8 = 1, DE = 1.414] Center --> N8[BottomRight Diag
D4 = 2, D8 = 1, DE = 1.414] style Center fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px style N5 fill:#FFB6C1 style N6 fill:#FFB6C1 style N7 fill:#FFB6C1 style N8 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` > [!NOTE] > The pink-highlighted diagonal neighbors are crucial: $D_4$ treats them as "far" (distance 2), $D_8$ treats them as "near" (distance 1), and $D_E$ treats them as "$\sqrt{2}$ away". This single difference governs whether diagonal pixel connectivity is allowed. ### 4.3 Shape Equidistance — Diamond vs Square vs Circle (Functional Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Plot pixels at D = constant from center] Start --> S1[D4 = 2] Start --> S2[D8 = 2] Start --> S3[DE = 2] S1 --> R1[Result: Diamond shape
4 pixels in cardinal directions] S2 --> R2[Result: Square shape
All pixels in 5x5 region] S3 --> R3[Result: Circle shape
Approx 12 pixels on circumference] R1 --> App1[Used in 4-connected morphology] R2 --> App2[Used in 8-connected morphology] R3 --> App3[Used in true geometric algorithms] style R1 fill:#87CEEB style R2 fill:#90EE90 style R3 fill:#FFA07A ``` ### 4.4 Decision Diagram — Which Distance Should You Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q{Need true geometric accuracy?} Q -->|Yes| Q1{Need rotation invariance?} Q -->|No| Q2{Need integer values?} Q1 -->|Yes| R1[Use D_E Euclidean] Q1 -->|No| R3[Use D_QE Quasi-Euclidean] Q2 -->|Yes, 4-conn| R2[Use D_4 City Block] Q2 -->|Yes, 8-conn| R4[Use D_8 Chessboard] Q2 -->|No, need speed| R5[Use Chamfer 3-4 or 5-7-11] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define the term "distance measure" in the context of digital image processing. List the four axioms that any valid distance metric must satisfy.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** A **distance measure** $D(p, q)$ is a real-valued function that assigns a non-negative scalar to any pair of pixels $p$ and $q$ in a digital image, representing their spatial separation. It must satisfy the following four metric axioms: 1. **Non-negativity:** $D(p, q) \geq 0$ for all $p, q$ 2. **Identity:** $D(p, q) = 0$ if and only if $p = q$ 3. **Symmetry:** $D(p, q) = D(q, p)$ for all $p, q$ 4. **Triangle Inequality:** $D(p, r) \leq D(p, q) + D(q, r)$ for all $p, q, r$ *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for stating axioms + 0.5 mark for each pair of axioms explained = 3 marks)* --- > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Q2. Differentiate between City Block distance ($D_4$) and Chessboard distance ($D_8$) with suitable formulas and diagrams.** *(3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **City Block Distance $D_4$** | **Chessboard Distance $D_8$** | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Formula | $\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert$ | $\max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert)$ | | Connectivity | 4-connected (no diagonals) | 8-connected (diagonals allowed) | | Shape at $D \leq 2$ | Diamond (rhombus) | Square (axis-aligned) | | Diagonal neighbors | At distance 2 | At distance 1 | | Movement | Horizontal + Vertical steps only | King-like (any of 8 directions) | The diamond shape of $D_4$ has 4 cardinal directions prominent, while $D_8$ produces a complete 5x5 square. $D_8$ is the "looser" connectivity, allowing diagonal jumps to be counted as 1 step. --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 1 Choice]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain the following distance measures with formulas and the equidistant shape traced by each: (i) Euclidean distance, (ii) City Block distance ($D_4$), (iii) Chessboard distance ($D_8$). *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **(i) Euclidean Distance ($D_E$):** This is the true geometric straight-line distance between two pixels, derived from the Pythagorean theorem. $$ D_E(p, q) = \sqrt{(x_1 - x_2)^2 + (y_1 - y_2)^2} $$ The set of pixels at $D_E \leq k$ traces a **circle** of radius $k$ around $p$. It is rotation-invariant but produces non-integer (irrational) values for diagonal separations. **(ii) City Block Distance ($D_4$):** Also called the Manhattan or taxicab distance, this measures the distance as the sum of absolute differences in coordinates. $$ D_4(p, q) = \vert x_1 - x_2 \vert + \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert $$ Pixels with $D_4 \leq 2$ form a **diamond** (rhombus) shape. The 4-connected neighbors of a pixel are all at $D_4 = 1$. Diagonal neighbors are at $D_4 = 2$. **(iii) Chessboard Distance ($D_8$):** Also called the Chebyshev distance, this takes the maximum of the absolute coordinate differences. $$ D_8(p, q) = \max(\vert x_1 - x_2 \vert, \vert y_1 - y_2 \vert) $$ Pixels with $D_8 \leq 2$ form a **square** (axis-aligned). All 8 neighbors (4 cardinal + 4 diagonal) of a pixel are at $D_8 = 1$. It corresponds to the king-move in chess. --- **(b)** For the center pixel $p = (4, 4)$ in a digital image, compute $D_4$, $D_8$, $D_E$, and Quasi-Euclidean distance to the following pixels: $q_1 = (4, 6)$, $q_2 = (7, 5)$, $q_3 = (1, 2)$, $q_4 = (6, 8)$. Compare the results in a tabular form and state which distance measure best approximates the Euclidean distance. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute $\Delta x = \vert 4 - x_q \vert$ and $\Delta y = \vert 4 - y_q \vert$ for each target. **Step 2:** For $q_1 = (4, 6)$: $$ \Delta x = 0, \quad \Delta y = 2 $$ $$ D_4 = 0 + 2 = 2, \quad D_8 = \max(0, 2) = 2, \quad D_E = \sqrt{0 + 4} = 2.000 $$ **Step 3:** For $q_2 = (7, 5)$: $$ \Delta x = 3, \quad \Delta y = 1 $$ $$ D_4 = 3 + 1 = 4, \quad D_8 = \max(3, 1) = 3, \quad D_E = \sqrt{9 + 1} = \sqrt{10} \approx 3.162 $$ $$ D_{QE} = 1 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 3 = 1 + 1.2426 = 2.243 $$ **Step 4:** For $q_3 = (1, 2)$: $$ \Delta x = 3, \quad \Delta y = 2 $$ $$ D_4 = 3 + 2 = 5, \quad D_8 = \max(3, 2) = 3, \quad D_E = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.606 $$ $$ D_{QE} = 2 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 3 = 2 + 1.2426 = 3.243 $$ **Step 5:** For $q_4 = (6, 8)$: $$ \Delta x = 2, \quad \Delta y = 4 $$ $$ D_4 = 2 + 4 = 6, \quad D_8 = \max(2, 4) = 4, \quad D_E = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.472 $$ $$ D_{QE} = 2 + (\sqrt{2} - 1) \cdot 4 = 2 + 1.657 = 3.657 $$ **Final Comparison Table:** | **Target** | $D_4$ | $D_8$ | $D_{QE}$ | $D_E$ (True) | Best Approx. | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | $q_1 = (4, 6)$ | 2 | 2 | 2.000 | 2.000 | All equal (pure axial) | | $q_2 = (7, 5)$ | 4 | 3 | 2.243 | 3.162 | $D_8$ (under), $D_4$ (over) | | $q_3 = (1, 2)$ | 5 | 3 | 3.243 | 3.606 | $D_8$ closest, $D_{QE}$ closer | | $q_4 = (6, 8)$ | 6 | 4 | 3.657 | 4.472 | $D_8$ closest, $D_{QE}$ close | **Conclusion:** $D_4$ systematically **overestimates** Euclidean distance (diamond shape is too "pointy"), while $D_8$ systematically **underestimates** it (square shape is too "fat"). The **Quasi-Euclidean distance** is the closest integer-friendly approximation overall. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** What is a Chamfer distance? Explain the 3-4 and 5-7-11 Chamfer masks. Compare them with Euclidean distance in terms of accuracy and computational cost. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Chamfer Distance Definition:** A chamfer distance is a fast, integer-weighted approximation of the Euclidean distance used in BFS-based distance transforms. It assigns integer weights to horizontal/vertical (short) and diagonal (long) moves between pixels. **3-4 Chamfer Mask:** Weights are **3** for horizontal/vertical steps and **4** for diagonal steps. The total distance is: $$ D_{3-4}(p, q) = 3 \cdot (\text{# H/V steps}) + 4 \cdot (\text{# diagonal steps}) $$ The maximum approximation error against $D_E$ is approximately **8%**. It is the fastest of the chamfer variants. **5-7-11 Chamfer Mask:** Weights are **5** for H/V, **7** for diagonal, and **11** for knight-moves (2-1 L-shaped jumps). The recurrence uses a 5x5 neighborhood instead of 3x3. $$ D_{5-7-11}(p, q) = 5 \cdot a + 7 \cdot b + 11 \cdot c $$ The maximum approximation error against $D_E$ is reduced to approximately **2%**, but the computational cost is higher due to the larger neighborhood. **Comparison Table:** | **Aspect** | $D_E$ | 3-4 Chamfer | 5-7-11 Chamfer | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Max error | 0% | ~8% | ~2% | | Integer-only? | No (sqrt needed) | Yes | Yes | | Neighborhood size | 1 (just target) | 3x3 | 5x5 | | Operations needed | sqrt | additions | additions | | Speed | Slow | Very fast | Fast | --- **(b)** A 5x5 binary image segment is given. Pixels with value 1 are: (1,1), (1,3), (2,4), (3,2), (4,4), (5,1), (5,5). Compute the **Chamfer 3-4 distance transform** from the pixel (1,1) using a two-pass BFS algorithm. Show the distance values for all pixels in the grid. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Initialize the grid** with $D = 0$ at the seed pixel $(1,1)$ and $D = \infty$ everywhere else. **Step 2: Forward pass (left-to-right, top-to-bottom)** — apply the recurrence with weights 3 (H/V) and 4 (diagonal). For pixel $(1,2)$: $$ D(1,2) = \min(D(1,1) + 3) = 0 + 3 = 3 $$ For pixel $(1,3)$: $$ D(1,3) = \min(D(1,2) + 3) = 3 + 3 = 6 $$ For pixel $(2,4)$: $$ D(2,4) = \min(D(1,3) + 4, D(1,4) + 3) = 6 + 4 = 10 $$ For pixel $(3,2)$: $$ D(3,2) = \min(D(2,1) + 3, D(2,2) + 3) = 3 + 3 = 6 $$ For pixel $(4,4)$: $$ D(4,4) = \min(D(3,3) + 4, D(3,4) + 3) = 9 + 4 = 13 $$ For pixel $(5,5)$: $$ D(5,5) = \min(D(4,4) + 4, D(4,5) + 3) = 13 + 4 = 17 $$ **Step 3: Backward pass** — refine by checking the lower-right neighbors and updating if a shorter path is found. (The process continues until no updates occur.) **Final 5x5 Chamfer 3-4 Distance Grid (from seed (1,1)):** | **Col\Row** | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** | **5** | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Row 1** | **0** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 12 | | **Row 2** | 3 | 6 | 9 | 10 | 13 | | **Row 3** | 6 | **6** | 9 | 12 | 15 | | **Row 4** | 9 | 9 | 12 | **13** | 16 | | **Row 5** | 12 | 12 | 15 | 16 | **17** | **Verification:** The seed $(1,1)$ has $D = 0$, as expected. The farthest pixel $(5,5)$ has $D = 17$, corresponding to a path of approximately $5$ diagonal-equivalent steps with chamfer weighting. Compared to true Euclidean distance $\sqrt{16+16} = \sqrt{32} \approx 5.66$, the chamfer value $17$ represents a weighted integer approximation. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:** > 1. **Formula confusion:** Students often confuse $D_4$ and $D_8$ formulas. Remember: **$D_4$ = SUM** of $\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$, **$D_8$ = MAX** of $\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if swapped. > 2. **Forgetting metric axioms:** When asked to "define a distance measure," simply giving a formula is INSUFFICIENT for full marks. You MUST list the four axioms: non-negativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality. > 3. **Shape diagrams:** Examiners explicitly look for the equidistant shape (diamond for $D_4$, square for $D_8$, circle for $D_E$). Drawing these earns you 1–2 easy marks. > 4. **Mixing up neighborhoods:** $D_4$ is the 4-connected (cardinal) neighborhood distance; $D_8$ is the 8-connected (cardinal + diagonal) neighborhood distance. Do not state the opposite. > 5. **Chamfer weights:** The 3-4 mask has weights 3 and 4 — NOT 4 and 3. The lower weight goes to the SHORTER (horizontal/vertical) move. > 6. **Quasi-Euclidean formulas:** Do NOT write the Quasi-Euclidean as a single unified formula. It is piecewise: use true horizontal cost when $\Delta x \geq \Delta y$, and vice versa. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Distance Measures in DIP** - [x] A **distance measure** is a scalar function $D(p, q)$ that must satisfy **non-negativity, identity, symmetry, and triangle inequality**. - [x] The **Minkowski family** of distances generalizes all three core measures via the parameter $r$. - [x] **City Block distance** ($D_4$): $\vert \Delta x \vert + \vert \Delta y \vert$ — sum of absolute differences; **diamond** shape; 4-connected. - [x] **Chessboard distance** ($D_8$): $\max(\vert \Delta x \vert, \vert \Delta y \vert)$ — maximum of absolute differences; **square** shape; 8-connected. - [x] **Euclidean distance** ($D_E$): $\sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ — true Pythagorean; **circle** shape; rotation-invariant. - [x] **Quasi-Euclidean** uses a hybrid: minimum of horizontal/vertical + diagonal geometric paths. - [x] **Chamfer 3-4** uses weights (3, 4) and approximates $D_E$ with max error ≈ 8% using only integer arithmetic. - [x] **Chamfer 5-7-11** uses weights (5, 7, 11) with a 5x5 neighborhood; max error ≈ 2% but more expensive. - [x] $D_4$ **overestimates** $D_E$; $D_8$ **underestimates** $D_E$; Quasi-Euclidean and Chamfer are the best **integer-friendly approximations**. - [x] Distance measures are foundational for **morphological operations, segmentation, distance transforms, and connected component labeling**. - [x] The **number** in $D_4$ and $D_8$ corresponds to the **connectivity order** of the pixel neighborhood (4 cardinal vs. 8 surrounding pixels).

topics.title: Simple Image Formation Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET746 - DIGITAL IMAGE PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Image Formation Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Simple Image Formation Model** is the fundamental mathematical framework used in Digital Image Processing (DIP) to represent how a two-dimensional digital image is created from a real-world three-dimensional scene. According to the standard KTU 2024 syllabus (aligned with Gonzalez & Woods, *Digital Image Processing*), the monochromatic (single-channel, grayscale) image formation model is expressed as:

$$f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$$

where:
- **$f(x, y)$** = Image intensity (gray level) at spatial coordinates $(x, y)$
- **$i(x, y)$** = Illumination component — the amount of light incident on the scene
- **$r(x, y)$** = Reflectance component — the amount of light reflected by the object

The illumination is bounded by $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$, and the reflectance is bounded by $0 < r(x, y) < 1$ (i.e., $0$ = total absorption / black, $1$ = total reflection / white).

The observed gray-level range is constrained as:

$$L_{\min} \leq f(x, y) \leq L_{\max}$$

For an **8-bit image**, $L_{\min} = 0$ (black) and $L_{\max} = 255$ (white). For a **general $k$-bit image**, $L_{\max} = 2^k - 1$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The product model $f(x, y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$ is a **multiplicative model**, not additive. This is a frequently tested concept in Part A questions. The illumination is conceptually analogous to "how much light is available" and reflectance to "how much of that light the object bounces back."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are taking a photograph of a red apple in your garden at sunset.

- The **sunset light** that falls on the apple = **$i(x, y)$ (illumination)**. If it's a bright noon, $i$ is large; if it's a dark evening, $i$ is small.
- The **apple's skin** naturally reflects only the red wavelengths and absorbs the rest. The fraction of light it reflects = **$r(x, y)$ (reflectance)**. For a shiny red apple, $r \approx 0.8$ (in red band); for a black T-shirt, $r \approx 0.05$.
- The **photograph you see** = product of the two. If the sun is dim *and* the apple is dark, the photo will be very dark (low $f$). If the sun is bright *and* the apple is shiny, the photo will be bright (high $f$).

**Real-world mapping:** This is exactly how your **digital camera sensor** records an image — each pixel's intensity is essentially (light hitting the sensor) × (sensor's quantum efficiency / response).

## 1.3 Related KTU Module-1 Concepts (Image Acquisition Pipeline)

The simple image formation model is part of a broader image acquisition chain:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Module 1 Reading Order:**
> 1. Image formation model → $f(x, y) = i(x,y) \cdot r(x,y)$
> 2. Image sensing (sensors, CCD/CMOS) — converts $f$ to electrical signal
> 3. Sampling — discretizes spatial $(x, y)$ coordinates
> 4. Quantization — discretizes intensity values
> 5. Pixel relationships (neighbors, adjacency, connectivity, distance)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of the Model

### Step 1: Light Source Interaction
A physical scene is illuminated by a light source. The incident illumination $i(x, y)$ is a continuous function over the scene coordinates. Mathematically it is treated as a deterministic, slowly varying function.

### Step 2: Object Reflectance Property
Each point in the scene has a reflectance property $r(x, y)$ determined by the material's surface characteristics. Reflectance is bounded between $0$ (perfect absorber — coal) and $1$ (perfect reflector — Lambertian white surface under ideal conditions).

### Step 3: Multiplicative Combination
The image sensor at position $(x, y)$ records a value proportional to the product of the two. This **multiplicative nature** is what makes intensity-based image processing (like homomorphic filtering later in the course) use logarithmic transforms:

$$\ln f(x, y) = \ln i(x, y) + \ln r(x, y)$$

The product becomes a sum in the log domain — this is a *very high-yield KTU point* for later modules.

### Step 4: Range Determination
The combined gray level must lie within the sensor's dynamic range $[L_{\min}, L_{\max}]$. For typical 8-bit systems, this is $[0, 255]$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Range** |
|---|---|---|
| $f(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y)$ | Simple image formation model | Dimensionless intensity |
| $0 < i(x, y) < \infty$ | Illumination bounds | Lumens / m² (irradiance) |
| $0 \leq r(x, y) \leq 1$ | Reflectance bounds | Dimensionless ratio |
| $L_{\min} \leq f(x, y) \leq L_{\max}$ | Sensor dynamic range | Gray levels |
| $L_{\max} = 2^k - 1$ | Max gray level for $k$-bit image | Integer |
| $N = 2^k$ | Number of gray levels | Integer |
| $\ln f = \ln i + \ln r$ | Log-domain linearization | For homomorphic filtering |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This model is the cornerstone of **homomorphic filtering** (a frequency-domain technique taught later in the KTU syllabus) used for illumination normalization in medical imaging, satellite imaging, and face recognition preprocessing.

## 2.3 Why Multiplicative and Not Additive?

In an ideal Lambertian scene under uniform illumination, the response is multiplicative because the photons reflected by the object are *proportional* to the photons incident on it. Real sensors do add **noise** $\eta(x, y)$:

$$g(x, y) = f(x, y) + \eta(x, y)$$

where $\eta$ is typically modeled as Gaussian or Poisson noise. The simple model assumes $\eta = 0$.

## 2.4 Image as a 2-D Function

A digital image is treated as a discrete function $f(x, y)$:
- **$x$** → column index (horizontal)
- **$y$** → row index (vertical)
- **$f$** → intensity (scalar for grayscale, vector for color)

Origin convention: KTU/Gonzalez convention places origin at the **top-left** corner, with $x$ increasing to the right and $y$ increasing downward.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Continuous Illumination to Discrete Pixel Value

The image formation pipeline can be derived rigorously:

### Stage A — Continuous Image Formation

A real-world scene is illuminated and forms a continuous intensity field. The continuous image function is:

$$f_c(x, y) = i(x, y) \cdot r(x, y), \quad x, y \in \mathbb{R}^2$$

This is the **analog image** — a 2-D continuous signal of infinite resolution.

### Stage B — Sampling (Spatial Discretization)

The continuous image is sampled at equally-spaced grid points. The sampling rate determines spatial resolution:

$$f_d(m, n) = f_c(m \Delta x, n \Delta y)$$

where:
- $m, n \in \mathbb{Z}$ are integer pixel indices
- $\Delta x, \Delta y$ are the sampling intervals (in mm or pixels/inch)
- Total image size: $M \times N$ pixels

**Nyquist Sampling Criterion** must be satisfied:

$$\Delta x \leq \frac{1}{2 f_{x,\max}}, \quad \Delta y \leq \frac{1}{2 f_{y,\max}}$$

where $f_{x,\max}$ and $f_{y,\max}$ are the maximum spatial frequencies in the image.

### Stage C — Quantization (Intensity Discretization)

Each sampled value $f_d(m, n)$ is mapped to the nearest discrete gray level:

$$f_q(m, n) = \text{round}\left[ \frac{f_d(m, n) - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} - L_{\min}} \cdot (2^k - 1) \right]$$

**Derivation details:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_q(m, n) &= \text{round}\left[ \frac{f_d(m, n) - L_{\min}}{L_{\max} - L_{\min}} \cdot (2^k - 1) \right] \\
&= \text{round}\left[ \frac{f_d(m, n) - 0}{255 - 0} \cdot 255 \right] \quad \text{(for 8-bit, } L_{\min}=0, L_{\max}=255) \\
&= \text{round}\left[ f_d(m, n) \cdot 1 \right] \\
&= \text{round}\left[ f_d(m, n) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

The **quantization step size** (resolution per gray level):

$$\Delta q = \frac{L_{\max} - L_{\min}}{2^k - 1}$$

For an 8-bit image, $\Delta q = 255/255 = 1$ gray level per step.

### Stage D — Total Storage and Information

Total number of bits to store a grayscale image:

$$B = M \times N \times k \text{ bits}$$

For a $1024 \times 1024$ 8-bit image:

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 \\
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \times 8 \\
&= 8{,}388{,}608 \text{ bits} \\
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \\
&\approx 1 \text{ MB}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU-style)

> **Problem:** A grayscale image is acquired with illumination varying between 50 and 200 lumens/m² across the scene. The objects have reflectance between 0.1 and 0.7. The sensor uses 6 bits per pixel. Find the minimum and maximum possible pixel values.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**
- $i_{\min} = 50$, $i_{\max} = 200$
- $r_{\min} = 0.1$, $r_{\max} = 0.7$
- $k = 6$ bits, so $L_{\max} = 2^6 - 1 = 63$

**Step 2 — Compute minimum intensity (darkest pixel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\min} &= i_{\min} \cdot r_{\min} \\
&= 50 \cdot 0.1 \\
&= 5.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum intensity (brightest pixel):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= i_{\max} \cdot r_{\max} \\
&= 200 \cdot 0.7 \\
&= 140.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Quantize to 6-bit range $[0, 63]$:**

The sensor's dynamic range doesn't fit both 5 and 140 — so the sensor will **clip** values below 0 to 0 and above 63 to 63. The usable pixel range is therefore **$[0, 63]$** with saturation at both ends. Most image data will be **saturated to 63** since the natural $f$ ranges from 5 to 140, well above the 6-bit cap.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The sensor is under-specified for this scene. A higher bit-depth (e.g., 8-bit, $L_{\max} = 255$) is needed. This is why camera sensors in bright outdoor scenes need ≥ 10–12 bits per channel.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Image Formation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Illumination i x y]
    A --> D[Object Surface]
    D --> E[Reflectance r x y]
    C --> F[Multiplicative Product f = i times r]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Image Sensor CCD or CMOS]
    G --> H[Sampling Spatial Discretization]
    H --> I[Quantization Intensity Discretization]
    I --> J[Digital Image f m n 2D Array]
    J --> K[Storage M by N by k bits]
    J --> L[Display Monitor]
```
